(g) Prove that for every \(c \in R\), every rational number \(r\), and every \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\),

    \[ |c\langle x, y\rangle-\langle c x, y\rangle|=|(c-r)\langle x, y\rangle-\langle(c-r) x, y\rangle| \leq 2|c-r|\|x\|\|y\| \]

    (h) Use the fact that for any \(c \in R,|c-r|\) can be made arbitrarily small, where \(r\) varies over the set of rational numbers, to establish item (b) of the definition of inner product. 28. Let \(V\) be a complex inner product space with an inner product \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\). Let \([\cdot, \cdot]\) be the real-valued function such that \([x, y]\) is the real part of the complex number \(\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Prove that \([\cdot, \cdot]\) is an inner product for V , where V is regarded as a vector space over \(R\). Prove, furthermore, that \([x, i x]=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). 29. Let \(V\) be a vector space over \(C\), and suppose that \([\cdot, \cdot]\) is a real inner product on \(V\), where \(V\) is regarded as a vector space over \(R\), such that \([x, i x]=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Let \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) be the complex-valued function defined by

    \[ \langle x, y\rangle=[x, y]+i[x, i y] \quad \text { for } x, y \in \mathrm{~V} . \]

    Prove that \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) is a complex inner product on \(V\). 30. Let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a norm (as defined in Exercise 24) on a complex vector space \(V\) satisfying the parallelogram law given in Exercise 11. Prove that there is an inner product \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) on V such that \(\|x\|^{2}=\langle x, x\rangle\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Hint: Apply Exercise 27 to \(V\) regarded as a vector space over \(R\). Then apply Exercise 29.

    6.2 THE GRAM-SCHMIDT ORTHOGONALIZATION PROCESS AND ORTHOGONAL COMPLEMENTS¶

    In previous chapters, we have seen the special role of the standard ordered bases for \(C^{n}\) and \(\mathrm{R}^{n}\). The special properties of these bases stem from the fact that the basis vectors form an orthonormal set. Just as bases are the building blocks of vector spaces. bases that are also orthonormal sets are the building blocks of imer product spaces. We now name such bases.

    Definition. Let V be an inner product space. A subset of V is an orthonormal basis for V if it is an ordered basis that is orthonormal.

    Example 1¶

    The standard ordered basis for \(F^{n}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(F^{n}\).

    Example 2¶

    The set

    \[ \left\{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right),\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\right\} \]

    is an orthonormal basis for \(R^{2}\).

    The next theorem and its corollaries illustrate why orthonormal sets and. in particular, orthonormal bases are so important.

    Theorem 6.3. Let \(\vee\) be an inner product space and \(S=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) be an orthogonal subset of \(\vee\) consisting of nonzero vectors. If \(y \in \operatorname{span}(S)\), then

    \[ y=\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}} v_{i} \]

    Proof. Write \(y=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}\), where \(a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k} \in F\). Then, for \(1 \leq j \leq k\), we have

    \[ \left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle=\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i}\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=a_{j}\left\langle v_{j}, v_{j}\right\rangle=a_{j}\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2} . \]

    So \(a_{j}=\frac{\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}}\), and the result follows. The next corollary follows immediately from Theorem 6.3. Corollary 1. If, in addition to the hypotheses of Theorem 6.3, \(S\) is orthonormal and \(y \in \operatorname{span}(S)\), then

    \[ y=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} . \]

    If \(V\) possesses a finite orthonormal basis, then Corollary 1 allows us to compute the coefficients in a linear combination very easily. (Sce Example 3.)

    Corollary 2. Let V be an inner product space, and let \(S\) be an orthogonal subset of \(V\) consisting of nonzero vectors. Then \(S\) is lincarly independent.

    Proof. Suppose that \(v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k} \in S\) and

    \[ \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_{i} v_{i}=0 \]

    As in the proof of Theorem 6.3 with \(y=0\), we have \(a_{j}=\left\langle 0, v_{j}\right\rangle /\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}=0\) for all \(j\). So \(S\) is linearly independent.

    Example 3¶

    By Corollary 2, the orthonormal set

    \[ \left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1,0), \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,-1,1), \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-1,1,2)\right\} \]

    obtained in Example 8 of Section 6.1 is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\). Let \(x=(2,1,3)\). The coefficients given by Corollary 1 to Theorem 6.3 that express \(x\) as a linear combination of the basis vectors are

    \[ a_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2+1)=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad a_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(2-1+3)=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}, \]

    and

    \[ a_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(-2+1+6)=\frac{5}{\sqrt{6}} . \]

    As a check, we have

    \[ (2.1,3)=\frac{3}{2}(1,1,0)+\frac{4}{3}(1,-1,1)+\frac{5}{6}(-1,1,2) . \]

    Corollary 2 tells us that the vector space \(H\) in Section 6.1 contains an infinite linearly independent set, and hence H is not a finite-dimensional vector space.

    Of course, we have not yet shown that every finitc-dimensional inner product space possesses an orthonormal basis. The next theorem takes us most of the way in obtaining this result. It tells us how to construct an orthogonal set from a linearly independent set of vectors in such a way that both sets generate the same subspace.

    Before stating this theorem, let us consider a simple case. Suppose that \(\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}^{\prime}\right\}\) is a linearly independent subset of an inner product space (and hence a basis for some two-dimensional subspace). We want to construct an orthogonal set from \(\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}\right\}\) that spans the same subspace. Figure 6.1 suggests that the set \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}\), where \(v_{1}=w_{1}\) and \(v_{2}=w_{2}-c w_{1}\), has this property if \(c\) is chosen so that \(v_{2}\) is orthogonal to \(W_{1}\).

    To find \(c\), we need only solve the following equation:

    \[ 0=\left\langle v_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{2}-c w_{1}, w_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle-c\left\langle w_{1}, w_{1}\right\rangle \]

    So

    \[ c=\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|w_{1}\right\|^{2}} . \]

    Thus

    \[ v_{2}=w_{2}-\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|w_{1}\right\|^{2}} w_{1} \]

    Figure 6.1

    The next theorem shows us that this process can be extended to any finite linearly independent subset.

    Theorem 6.4. Let \(\vee\) be an innor product space and \(S=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}\) be a lincarly independent subset of V . Define \(S^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\), where \(v_{1}=w_{1}\) and

    \[ \begin{equation*} v_{k}=w_{k}-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}} v_{j} \quad \text { for } 2 \leq k \leq n \tag{1} \end{equation*} \]

    Then \(S^{\prime}\) is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors such that \(\operatorname{span}\left(S^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}(S)\). Proof. The proof is by mathematical induction on \(n\), the number of vectors in \(S\). For \(k=1,2, \ldots, n\), let \(S_{k}=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{k}\right\}\). If \(n=1\), then the theorem is proved by taking \(S_{1}^{\prime}=S_{1}\); i.e., \(v_{1}=w_{1} \neq 0\). Assume then that the set \(S_{k-1}^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k-1}\right\}\) with the desired properties has been constructed by the repeated use of (1). We show that the set \(S_{k}^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k-1}, v_{k}\right\}\) also has the desired propertics, where \(v_{k}\) is oltained from \(S_{k-1}^{\prime}\) by (1). If \(v_{k}=\) 0 . then (1) implies that \(u_{k} \in \operatorname{span}\left(S_{k-1}^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k-1}\right)\), which contradicts the assumption that \(S_{k}\) is linearly independent. For \(1 \leq i \leq k-1\), it follows from (1) that

    \[ \left\langle v_{k:}, v_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle w_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{j}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}}\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle u_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle-\frac{\left\langle w_{k}, v_{i}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}}\left\|v_{i}\right\|^{2}=0 \]

    since \(\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle=0\) if \(i \neq j\) by the induction assumption that \(S_{k-1}^{\prime}\) is orthogonal. Hence \(S_{k}^{\prime}\) is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors. Now, by (1), we have that \(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right) \subseteq \operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)\). But by Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.3, \(S_{k}^{\prime}\) is linearly independent; so \(\operatorname{dim}\left(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right)\right)=\operatorname{dim}\left(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)\right)=k\). Therefore \(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}^{\prime}\right)=\) \(\operatorname{span}\left(S_{k}\right)\).

    The construction of \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) by the use of Theorem 6.4 is called the Gram-Schmidt process.

    Example 4¶

    In \(R^{4}\), let \(u_{1}=(1,0,1,0)\). \(u_{2}^{\prime}=(1,1,1,1)\), and \(u_{3}=(0,1,2,1)\). Then \(\left\{w_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}\) is linearly independent. We use the Gram Schmidt process to compute the orthogonal vectors \(v_{1}, v_{2}\), and \(v_{3}\), and then wo normalize these vectors to obtain an orthonormal set.

    Take \(v_{1}=w_{1}=(1,0,1,0)\). Then

    \[ \begin{aligned} v_{2} & =u_{2}-\frac{\left\langle w_{2}, c_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} l_{1} \\ & =(1,1,1,1)-\frac{2}{2}(1,0,1,0) \\ & =(0,1,0,1) . \end{aligned} \]

    Finally:

    \[ \begin{aligned} v_{3} & =w_{3}-\frac{\left\langle w_{3} \cdot v_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} v_{1}-\frac{\left\langle w_{3}, v_{2}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{2}\right\|^{2}} v_{2} \\ & =(0.1,2,1)-\frac{2}{2}(1,0,1.0)-\frac{2}{2}(0,1,0.1) \\ & =(-1,0,1,0) . \end{aligned} \]

    These vectors can be normalized to obtain the orthonormal basis \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}\), where

    \[ \begin{aligned} & u_{1}=\frac{1}{\left\|r_{1}\right\|} v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,0,1,0), \\ & u_{2}=\frac{1}{\left\|r_{2}\right\|} v_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(0,1,0,1) . \end{aligned} \]

    and

    \[ u_{3}=\frac{v_{3}}{\left\|v_{3}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1.0,1,0) \]

    Example 5¶

    Let \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}(R)\) with the immer product \(\langle f(x): g(x)\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t\), and consider the subspace \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) with the standard ordered basis 3 . We use the Gram-Schmidt process to replace \(\beta\) by an orthogonal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\), and then use this orthogonal basis to obtain an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\).

    Take \(r_{1}=1\). Then \(\left\|r_{1}\right\|^{2}=\int_{-1}^{1} 1^{2} d t=2\), and \(\left\langle r, r_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t \cdot 1 d t=0\). Thus

    \[ v_{2}=x-\frac{\left\langle y_{1}, x\right\rangle}{\left\|c_{1}\right\|^{2}}=x-\frac{0}{2}=x \]

    Furthermore,

    \[ \left\langle x^{2}, v_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} \cdot 1 d t=\frac{2}{3} \quad \text { and } \quad\left\langle x^{2}, v_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} \cdot t d t=0 . \]

    Therefore

    \[ \begin{aligned} v_{3} & =x^{2}-\frac{\left\langle x^{2}, v_{1}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{1}\right\|^{2}} v_{1}-\frac{\left\langle x^{2}, v_{2}\right\rangle}{\left\|v_{2}\right\|^{2}} v_{2} \\ & =x^{2}-\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1-0 \cdot x \\ & =x^{2}-\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} \]

    We conclude that \(\left\{1, x, x^{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right\}\) is an orthogonal basis for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\). To obtain an orthonormal basis, we normalize \(v_{1}, v_{2}\), and \(v_{3}\) to obtain

    \[ \begin{aligned} & u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\int_{-1}^{1} 1^{2} d t}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & u_{2}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{\int_{-1}^{1} t^{2} d t}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x \end{aligned} \]

    and similarly,

    \[ u_{3}=\frac{v_{3}}{\left\|v_{3}\right\|}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right) \]

    Thus \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}\) is the desired orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\). If we continue applying the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process to the basis \(\left\{1, x, x^{2}, \ldots\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}(R)\), we obtain an orthogonal basis whose elements are called the Legendre polynomials. The orthogonal polynomials \(v_{1}, v_{2}\), and \(v_{3}\) in Example 5 are the first three Legendre polynomials.

    The following result gives us a simple method of representing a vector as a lincar combination of the vectors in an orthonormal basis.

    Theorem 6.5. Let \(V\) be a nonzero finite-dimensional inncr product space. Then \(V\) has an orthonormal basis \(\beta\). Furthermorc, if \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), then

    \[ x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} . \]

    Proof. Let \(\beta_{0}\) be an ordered basis for V. Apply Theorem 6.4 to obtain an orthogonal set \(\beta^{\prime}\) of nonzero vectors with \(\operatorname{span}\left(\beta^{\prime}\right)=\operatorname{span}\left(\beta_{0}\right)=\mathrm{V}\). By normalizing cach vector in \(\beta^{\prime}\), we obtain an orthonormal set \(\beta\) that generates V. By Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.3, \(\beta\) is linearly independent; therefore \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis for V . The remainder of the theorem follows from Corollary 1 to Theorem 6.3.

    Example 6¶

    We use Theorem 6.5 to represent the polynomial \(f(x)=1+2 x+3 x^{2}\) as a linear combination of the vectors in the orthonormal basis \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) obtained in Example 5 . Observe that

    \[ \begin{aligned} & \left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=2 \sqrt{2} \\ & \left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} t\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{3} \end{aligned} \]

    and

    \[ \left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 t^{2}-1\right)\left(1+2 t+3 t^{2}\right) d t=\frac{2 \sqrt{10}}{5} . \]

    Therefore \(f(x)=2 \sqrt{2} u_{1}+\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{3} u_{2}+\frac{2 \sqrt{10}}{5} u_{3}\). Theorem 6.5 gives us a simple method for computing the entries of the matrix representation of a linear operator with respect to an orthonormal basis.

    Corollary. Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space with an orthonormal basis \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\). Let \(T\) be a linear operator on V , and let \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{; j}\). Then for any \(i\) and \(j, A_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle\).

    Proof. From Theorem 6.5, we have

    \[ \mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} \]

    Hence \(A_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle\). The scalars \(\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle\) given in Theorem 6.5 have been studied extensively for special inner product spaces. Although the vectors \(v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\) were chosen from an orthonormal basis, we introduce a terminology associated with orthonormal sets \(\beta\) in more general inner product spaces.

    Definition. Let \(\beta\) be an orthonormal subset (possibly infinite) of an inner product space V , and let \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). We define the Fourier coefficients of \(x\) relative to \(\beta\) to be the scalars \(\langle x, y\rangle\), wherc \(y \in \beta\).

    In the first half of the 19th century, the French mathematician Jean Baptiste Fourier was associated with the study of the scalars

    \[ \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) \sin n t d t \text { and } \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) \cos n t d t \]

    or more generally,

    \[ c_{n}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) e^{-i n t} d t \]

    for a function \(f\). In the context of Example 9 of Section 6.1, we see that \(c_{n}=\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle\), where \(f_{n}(t)=e^{i n t}\); that is, \(c_{n}\) is the \(n\)th Fourier coefficient for a continuous function \(f \in \mathrm{~V}\) relative to \(S\). These coefficients are the "classical" Fourier cocfficients of a function, and the literature concerning the behavior of these coefficients is extensive. We learn more about these Fourier coefficients in the remainder of this chapter.

    Example 7¶

    Let \(S=\left\{e^{\text {int }}: n\right.\) is an integer \(\}\). In Example 9 of Section 6.1, \(S\) was shown to be an orthonormal set in H . We compute the Fouricr coefficients of \(f(t)=t\) relative to \(S\). Using integration by parts, we have, for \(n \neq 0\),

    \[ \left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \overline{t e^{i n t}} d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} t e^{-i n t} d t=\frac{-1}{i n} \]

    and, for \(n=0\),

    \[ \langle f, 1\rangle=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} t(1) d t=\pi \]

    As a result of these computations, and using Exercise 16 of this section, we obtain an upper bound for the sum of a special infinite serics as follows:

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|f\|^{2} & \geq \sum_{n=-k}^{-1}\left|\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2}+|\langle f, 1\rangle|^{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{k}\left|\left\langle f, f_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2} \\ & =\sum_{n=-k}^{-1} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \\ & =2 \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2} \end{aligned} \]

    for every \(k\). Now, using the fact that \(\|f\|^{2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi^{2}\), we obtain

    \[ \frac{4}{3} \pi^{2} \geq 2 \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}}+\pi^{2} \]

    or

    \[ \frac{\pi^{2}}{6} \geq \sum_{n=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \]

    Because this inequality holds for all \(k\), we may let \(k \rightarrow \infty\) to obtain

    \[ \frac{\pi^{2}}{6} \geq \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \]

    Additional results may be produced by replacing \(f\) by other functions. We are now ready to proceed with the concept of an orthogonal complement.

    Definition. Let \(S\) be a noncinpty subset of an inner product space V . We define \(S^{\perp}\) (read " \(S\) perp") to be the set of all vectors in \(V\) that are orthogonal to every vector in \(S\); that is, \(S^{\perp}=\{x \in \mathrm{~V}:\langle x, y\rangle=0\) for all \(y \in S\}\). The set \(S^{\perp}\) is called the orthogonal complement of \(S\).

    It is easily seen that, \(S^{\perp}\) is a subspace of V for any subset \(S\) of V .

    Example 8¶

    The reader should verify that \(\{0\}^{\perp}=V\) and \(V^{\perp}=\{0\}\) for any inner product space \(V\).

    Example 9¶

    If \(V=\mathrm{R}^{3}\) and \(S=\left\{e_{3}\right\}\), then \(S^{\perp}\) equals the \(x y\)-plane (see Exercise 5). Exercise 18 provides an interesting example of an orthogonal complement in an infinite-dimensional inner product space.

    Consider the problem in \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\) of finding the distance from a point \(P\) to a plane W. (See Figure 6.2.) Problems of this type arise in many settings. If we let \(y\) be the vector determined by 0 and \(P\), we may restate the problem as follows: Determine the vector \(u\) in \(W\) that is "closest" to \(y\). The desired distance is clearly given by \(\|y-u\|\). Notice from the figure that the vector \(z=y-u\) is orthogonal to every vector in W , and so \(z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\).

    The next result presents a practical method of finding \(u\) in the case that W is a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space.

    Figure 6.2

    Theorem 6.6. Let \(W\) be a finitc-dimensional subspace of an inner product space V , and let \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Then there exist unique vectors \(u \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\) such that \(y=u+z\). Furthermore, if \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for W , then

    \[ u=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} \]

    Proof. Let \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for W , let \(u\) be as defined in the preceding equation, and let \(z=y-u\). Clearly \(u \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(y=u+z\).

    To show that \(z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\), it suffices to show, by Exercise 7, that \(z\) is orthogonal to each \(v_{j}\). For any \(j\), we have

    \[ \begin{aligned} \left\langle z, v_{j}\right\rangle & =\left\langle\left(y-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle=\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle \\ & =\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle-\left\langle y, v_{j}\right\rangle=0 \end{aligned} \]

    To show uniqueness of \(u\) and \(z\), suppose that \(y=u+z=u^{\prime}+z^{\prime}\), where \(u^{\prime} \in \mathrm{W}\) and \(z^{\prime} \in \mathrm{W}^{\perp}\). Then \(u-u^{\prime}=z^{\prime}-z \in \mathrm{~W} \cap \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}=\{0\}\). Therefore. \(u=u^{\prime}\) and \(z=z^{\prime}\).

    Corollary. In the notation of Theorm 6.6. the vector \(u\) is the unique vector in W that is "closest" to \(y\); that is, for any \(x \in \mathrm{~W},\|y-x\| \geq\|y-u\|\). and this inequality is an equality if and only if \(x=u\).

    Proof. As in Theorem 6.6, we have that \(y=u+z\), where \(z \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\). Let \(x \in \mathrm{~W}\). Then \(u-x\) is orthogonal to \(z\), so, by Exercise 10 of Section 6.1 , we have

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|y-x\|^{2} & =\|u+z-x\|^{2}=\|(u-x)+z\|^{2}=\|u-x\|^{2}+\|z\|^{2} \\ & \geq\|z\|^{2}=\|y-u\|^{2} . \end{aligned} \]

    Now suppose that \(\|y-x\|=\|y-u\|\). Then the inequality above becomes an equality, and therefore \(\|u-x\|^{2}+\|z\|^{2}=\|z\|^{2}\). It follows that \(\|u-x\|=0\), and hence \(x=u\). The proof of the converse is obvious.

    The vector \(u\) in the corollary is called the orthogonal projection of \(y\) on W. We will see the importance of orthogonal projections of vectors in the application to least squares in Section 6.3.

    Example 10¶

    Let \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{3}(R)\) with the inner product

    \[ \langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t \quad \text { for all } f(x), g(x) \in \mathrm{V} \]

    We compute the orthogonal projection \(f_{1}(x)\) of \(f(x)=x^{3}\) on \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\). By Example 5,

    \[ \left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, u_{3}\right\}=\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x, \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right)\right\} \]

    is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\). For these vectors, we have

    \[ \left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} d t=0, \quad\left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} t d t=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{5} \]

    and

    \[ \left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} t^{3} \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 t^{2}-1\right) d t=0 \]

    Hence

    \[ f_{1}(x)=\left\langle f(x), u_{1}\right\rangle u_{1}+\left\langle f(x), u_{2}\right\rangle u_{2}+\left\langle f(x), u_{3}\right\rangle u_{3}=\frac{3}{5} x . \]

    It was shown (Corollary 2 to the replacement theorem, p. 47) that any linearly independent set in a finite-dimensional vector space can be extended to a basis. The next theorem provides an interesting analog for an orthonormal subset of a finite-dimensional inner product space.

    Theorem 6.7. Suppose that \(S=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) is an orthonormal set in an \(n\)-dimensional inner product space \(V\). Then (a) \(S\) can be extendcd to an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}, v_{k+1}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V . (b) If \(\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}(S)\), then \(S_{1}=\left\{v_{k+1}, v_{k+2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(W^{\perp}\) (using the preceding notation). (c) If \(W\) is any subspace of \(V\), then \(\operatorname{dim}(V)=\operatorname{dim}(W)+\operatorname{dim}\left(W^{\perp}\right)\).

    Proof. (a) By Corollary 2 to the replacement theorem (p. 47), \(S\) can be extended to an ordered basis \(S^{\prime}=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}, w_{k+1}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}\) for \(V\). Now apply the Gram-Schmidt process to \(S^{\prime}\). The first \(k\) vectors resulting from this process are the vectors in \(S\) by Exercise 8, and this new set spans V. Normalizing the last \(n-k\) vectors of this set produces an orthonormal set that spans \(V\). The result now follows. (b) Because \(S_{1}\) is a subset of a basis, it is linearly independent. Since \(S_{1}\) is clearly a subset of \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\), we need only show that it spans \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\). Note that, for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have

    \[ x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} . \]

    If \(x \in W_{-}^{\perp}\), then \(\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle=0\) for \(1 \leq i \leq k\). Therefore,

    \[ x=\sum_{i=k+1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle v_{i} \in \operatorname{span}\left(S_{1}\right) . \]

    (c) Let W be a subspace of V . It is a finite-dimensional inner product space because V is, and so it has an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\). By (a) and (b), we have

    \[ \operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{V})=n=k+(n-k)=\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{W})+\operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\right) \]

    Example 11¶

    Let \(W=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}\right\}\right)\) in \(F^{3}\). Then \(x=(a, b, c) \in W^{\perp}\) if and only if \(0=\) \(\left\langle x, e_{1}\right\rangle=a\) and \(0=\left\langle x, e_{2}\right\rangle=b\). So \(x=(0,0, c)\), and therefore \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}=\) span \(\left(\left\{e_{3}\right\}\right)\). One can deduce the same result by noting that \(e_{3} \in W^{\perp}\) and, from (c), that \(\operatorname{dim}\left(W^{\perp}\right)=3-2=1\).

    EXERCISES¶

    1. Label the following statements as true or false. (a) The Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process allows us to construct an orthonormal set from an arbitrary set of vectors. (b) Every nonzero finite-dimensional inner product space has an orthonormal basis. (c) The orthogonal complement of any set is a sulbspace. (d) If \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) is a basis for an inner product space V , then for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) the scalars \(\left\langle x^{\prime}, d_{i}\right\rangle\) are the Fourier coefficients of \(x\). (e) An orthonormal basis must be an ordered basis. (f) Every orthogonal set is linearly indepondent. (g) Every orthonormal set is linearly independent.
    2. In each part, apply the Gram Schmidt process to the given subset \(S\) of the inner product space V to obtain an orthogonal basis for \(\operatorname{span}(S)\). Then normalize the vectors in this basis to obtain an orthonormal basis ; 3 for \(\operatorname{span}(S)\), and compute the Fourier coeflicients of the given vector relative to 3 . Finally. use Theorem 6.5 to verify your result. (a) \(V=R^{3} . S=\{(1,0,1),(0.1,1),(1,3.3)\}\), and \(: r=(1,1,2)\) (b) \(V=R^{3} . S=\{(1,1,1),(0.1,1),(0.0,1)\}\), and \(: x=(1,0,1)\) (c) \(V=\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) with the imner product \(\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t\), \(S=\left\{1, x, x^{2}\right\}\), and \(h(x)=1+x\) (d) \(V=\operatorname{span}(S)\), where \(S=\{(1, i, 0),(1-i, 2,4 i)\}\), and \(r=(3+i .4 i,--1)\) (e) \(V=\mathrm{R}^{\cdot}, S=\{(2,-1,-2,4),(-2.1 .-5,5),(-1,3,7,11)\}\). and \(x=\) (-11.8. -4, 18) (f) \(\quad \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{1} . S=\{(1,-2,-1,3) .(3,6,3,-1),(1,4,2,8)\}\), and \(x=(-1,2,1,1)\) (g) \(V=M_{2 \times 2}(R), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{rr}3 & 5 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right),\left(\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 9 \\ 5 & -1\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{rr}7 & -17 \\ 2 & -6\end{array}\right)\right\}\), and \(A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}-1 & 27 \\ -4 & 8\end{array}\right)\) (h) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2 \times 2}(R), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{rr}11 & 1 \\ 2 & 5\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{ll}4 & -12 \\ 3 & -16\end{array}\right)\right\}\). and \(A=\) \(\left(\begin{array}{rr}8 & 6 \\ 25 & -13\end{array}\right)\) (i) \(V=\operatorname{span}(S)\) with the inmer product \(\langle f \cdot g\rangle=\int_{0}^{\pi} f(t) g(t) d t\), \(S=\{\sin t, \cos t, 1, t\}\), and \(h(t)=2 t+1\) (j) \(V=C^{4} . S=\{(1, i, 2-i,-1),(2+3 i .3 i, 1-i, 2 i)\), \((-1+7 i .6+10 i, 11-4 i, 3+4 i)\}\), and \(x=(-2+7 i .6+9 i .9-3 i, 4+4 i)\) (k) \(V=\mathrm{C}^{4}, S=\{(-4,3-2 i, i, 1-4 i)\), \((-1-5 i, 5-4 i,-3+5 i, 7-2 i) \cdot(-27-i,-7-6 i .-15+25 i,-7-6 i)\}\), and \(x=(-13-7 i,-12+3 i,-39-11 i,-26+3 i)\) (l) \(V=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(C): S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{cc}1-i & -2-3 i \\ 2+2 i & 4+i\end{array}\right) \cdot\left(\begin{array}{cc}8 i & 4 \\ -3-3 i & -4+4 i\end{array}\right)\right.\).
    \[ \left.\left(\begin{array}{cc} -25-38 i & -2-13 i \\ 12-78 i & -7+24 i \end{array}\right)\right\} . \text { and } A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -2+8 i & -13+i \\ 10-10 i & 9-9 i \end{array}\right) \]

    (m) \(V=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(C), S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1+i & -i \\ 2-i & 1+3 i\end{array}\right),\left(\begin{array}{cc}-1-7 i & -3-8 i \\ 1+10 i & -6-2 i\end{array}\right)\right.\),

    \[ \left.\left(\begin{array}{cc} -11-132 i & -34-31 i \\ 7-126 i & -71-5 i \end{array}\right)\right\}, \text { and } A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -7+5 i & 3+18 i \\ 9-6 i & -3+7 i \end{array}\right) \]
    1. In \(R^{2}\), let
    \[ \beta=\left\{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right),\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right\} . \]

    Find the Fourier coefficients of (3.4) relative to is. 4. Let \(S=\{(1,(), i),(1.2,1)\}\) in \(C^{3}\). Compute \(S^{\perp}\). 5. Let \(S_{0}=\left\{r_{0}\right\}\), where \(x_{0}\) is a nonzero vector in \(R^{3}\). Describe \(S_{0}^{\perp}\) geonetrically. Now suppose that \(S=\left\{x_{1}, x_{2}\right\}\) is a lincarly independent subset of \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\). Describe \(S^{\perp}\) geometrically. 6. Let V be an imer product space, and let W be a finite-dimensional subspace of V . If \(x \notin \mathrm{~W}\), prove that there exists \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\) such that \(y \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\), but \(\langle r, y\rangle \neq 0\). Hint: Use Theorem 6.(i. 7. Let \(\beta\) be a basis for a subspace \(W\) of an imer product space \(V\). and let \(z \in V\). Prove that \(z \in W^{\perp}\) if and only if \(\left\langle z, v^{\prime}\right\rangle=0\) for every \(u^{\prime} \in \beta\). 8. Prove that if \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}\right\}\) is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors, then the vectors \(r_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\) derived from the Gram Schmidt process satisfy \(r_{i}=u_{i}\) for \(i=1,2 \ldots, n\). Hint: Use mathematical induction. 9. Let \(W=\operatorname{span}(\{(i, 0,1)\})\) in \(C^{3}\). Find orthonormal bases for \(W\) and \(W^{\perp}\). 10. Let \(W\) be a finite-dimensional subspace of an imer product space \(V\). Prove that there exists a projection \(T\) on \(W\) along \(W^{\perp}\) that satisfies \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})=\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\). In addition. prove that \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\| \leq\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Hint: Use Theorem 6.6 and Exercise 10 of Section 6.1. (Projections are defined in the exercises of Section 2.1.) 11. Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) matrix with complex entrics. Prove that \(A A^{*}=I\) if and only if the rows of \(A\) form an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{C}^{n}\). 12. Prove that for any matrix \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F),\left(\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right)\right)^{\perp}=\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)\). 13. Let V be an inner product space, \(S\) and \(S_{0}\) be subscts of V , and W be a finite-dimensional subspace of V . Prove the following results. (a) \(S_{0} \subseteq S\) implies that \(S^{\perp} \subseteq S_{0}^{\perp}\). (b) \(S \subseteq\left(S^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}\); so span \((S) \subseteq\left(S^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}\). (c) \(W=\left(W^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}\). IIint: Use Exercise 6. (d) \(V=W \ominus W^{\perp}\). (See the exercises of Section 1.3.) 14. Let \(W_{1}\) and \(W_{2}\) be subspaces of a finite-dimensional inner product space. Prove that \(\left(W_{1}+W_{2}\right)^{\perp}=W_{1}^{\perp} \cap W_{2}^{\perp}\) and \(\left(W_{1} \cap W_{2}\right)^{\perp}=W_{1}^{\perp}+W_{2}^{\perp}\). (See the definition of the sum of subsets of a vector space on page 22.) Hint for the second equation: Apply Excrcise 13(c) to the first equation. 15. Let \(V\) be a finitc-dimensional inner product space over \(F\). (a) Parseval's Identity. Let \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for V . For any \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\) prove that

    \[ \langle x, y\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle \overline{\left\langle y, v_{i}\right\rangle} . \]

    (b) Use (a) to prove that if \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis for \(V\) with inner product \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\), then for any \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\)

    \[ \left\langle\phi_{\beta}(x), \phi_{\beta}(y)\right\rangle^{\prime}=\left\langle[x]_{\beta},[y]_{\beta}\right\rangle^{\prime}=\langle x, y\rangle, \]

    where \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle^{\prime}\) is the standard inner product on \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\). 16. (a) Bessel's Incquality. Let V be an inner product spacc, and let \(S=\) \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots v_{n}\right\}\) be an orthonormal subset of \(V\). Prove that for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) we have

    \[ \|x\|^{2} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\left\langle x, v_{i}\right\rangle\right|^{2} \]

    Iint: Apply Theorem 6.6 to \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}(S)\). Then use Exercise 10 of Section 6.1. (b) In the context of (a), prove that Bessel's inequality is an equality if and only if \(x \in \operatorname{span}(S)\). 17. Let T be a linear operator on an inner product space V . If \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=0\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), prove that \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\). In fact, prove this result if the equality holds for all \(x\) and \(y\) in some basis for V . 18. Let \(V=C([-1,1])\). Suppose that \(W_{e}\) and \(W_{o}\) denote the subspaces of \(V\) consisting of the even and odd functions, respectively. (Sec Exercise 22 of Section 1.3.) Prove that \(\mathrm{W}_{e}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{o}\), where the inner product on V is defined by

    \[ \langle f, g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t \]
    1. In each of the following parts, find the orthogonal projection of the given vector on the given subspace \(W\) of the inner product space \(V\). (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{2}, u=(2,6)\), and \(\mathrm{W}=\{(\dot{x}, y): y=4 x\}\). (b) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{3}, u=(2,1,3)\), and \(\mathrm{W}=\{(x, y, z): x+3 y-2 z=0\}\). (c) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}(R)\) with the inner product \(\langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t\), \(h(x)=4+3 x-2 x^{2}\), and \(\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\).
    2. In each part of Exercise 19, find the distance from the given vector to the subspace \(W\).
    3. Let \(V=\mathrm{C}([-1,1]\rangle\) with the inner product \(\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t\), and let \(W\) be the subspace \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\), viewed as a space of functions. Use the orthonormal basis obtained in Example 5 to compute the "best" (closest) second-degree polynomial approximation of the function \(h(t)=\) \(e^{t}\) on the interval \([-1,1]\).
    4. Let \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}([0,1])\) with the inner product \(\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t\). Let W be the subspace spanned by the linearly independent set \(\{t, \sqrt{t}\}\). (a) Find an orthonormal basis for W . (b) Let \(h(t)=t^{2}\). Use the orthonormal basis obtained in (a) to obtain the "best" (closest) approximation of \(h\) in W.
    5. Let V be the vector space defined in Example 5 of Section 1.2, the space of all sequences \(\sigma\) in \(F\) (where \(F=R\) or \(F=C\) ) such that \(\sigma(n) \neq 0\) for only finitely many positive integers \(n\). For \(\sigma, \mu \in \mathrm{V}\), we define \(\langle\sigma, \mu\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sigma(n) \overline{\mu(n)}\). Since all but a finite number of terms of the series are zero, the series converges. (a) Prove that \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) is an inner product on \(V\), and hence \(V\) is an inner product space. (b) For each positive integer \(n\), let \(e_{n}\) be the sequence defined by \(e_{n}(k)=\delta_{n, k}\), where \(\delta_{n, k}\) is the Kronecker delta. Prove that \(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(V\). (c) Let \(\sigma_{n}=e_{1}+e_{n}\) and \(\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{\sigma_{n}: n \geq 2\right\}\right.\). (i) Prove that \(e_{1} \notin \mathrm{~W}\), so \(\mathrm{W} \neq \mathrm{V}\). (ii) Prove that \(W^{\perp}=\{0\}\), and conclude that \(W \neq\left(W^{\perp}\right)^{\perp}\).

    Thus the assumption in Exercise 13(c) that W is finite-dimensional is essential.

    6.3 THE ADJOINT OF A LINEAR OPERATOR¶

    In Section 6.1, we defined the conjugate transpose \(A^{*}\) of a matrix \(A\). For a lincar operator \(T\) on an inner product space \(V\), we now define a related lincar operator on V called the adjoint of T , whose matrix representation with respect to any orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for V is \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{*}\). The analogy between conjugation of complex mumbers and adjoints of linear operators will become apparent. We first need a preliminary result.

    Lct V be an inner product space, and let \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\). The function \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) defined by \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle\) is clearly linear. More interesting is the fact that if V is finite-dimensional, every linear transformation from V into \(F\) is of this form.

    Theorem 6.8. Let \(\vee\) bc a finite-dimensional inner product space over \(F\), and let \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) be a linear transformation. Then there exists a unique vector \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\) such that \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\).

    Proof. Let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for V , and let

    \[ y=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{\mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)} v_{i} \]

    Define \(\mathrm{h}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) by \(\mathrm{h}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle\), which is clearly lincar. Furthermore, for: \(1 \leq j \leq n\) we have

    \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}\left(v_{j}\right)=\left\langle v_{j}, y\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{j}, \sum_{i=1}^{n} \overline{\mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)} v_{i}\right\rangle & =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right)\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle \\ & =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{~g}\left(v_{i}\right) \delta_{j i}=\mathrm{g}\left(v_{j}\right) . \end{aligned} \]

    Since g and h both agree on \(\beta\), we have that \(\mathrm{g}=\mathrm{h}\) by the corollary to Theorem 2.6 (p. 73).

    To show that \(y\) is unique, suppose that \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle\) for all \(x\). Then \(\langle x, y\rangle=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle\) for all \(x\); so by Theorem 6.1(e) (p. 333), we have \(y=y^{\prime}\).

    Example 1¶

    Define \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow R\) by \(\mathrm{g}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=2 a_{1}+a_{2}\); clearly g is a linear transformation. Let \(\beta=\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}\right\}\), and let \(y=\mathrm{g}\left(e_{1}\right) e_{1}+\mathrm{g}\left(e_{2}\right) e_{2}=2 e_{1}+e_{2}=(2,1)\), as in the proof of Theorem 6.8. Then \(g\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=\left\langle\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right),(2,1)\right\rangle=2 a_{1}+a_{2}\).

    Theorem 6.9. Let V be a finite-dimensional inner product space, and let T be a linear operator on V . Then there exists a mique function \(\mathrm{T}^{*}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) such that \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Furthermore. \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) is linear.

    Proof. Let \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Define \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) by \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). We first show that g is lincar. Let \(x_{1}, x_{2} \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(c \in F\). Then

    \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{g}\left(c x_{1}+x_{2}\right) & =\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(c x_{1}+x_{2}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle \\ & =r\left\langle\mathrm{~T}\left(x_{1}\right) \cdot y\right\rangle+\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2}\right), y\right\rangle=\operatorname{cg}\left(x_{1}\right)+\mathrm{g}\left(x_{2}\right) . \end{aligned} \]

    Hence \(g\) is linear. We now apply Theorem 6.8 to obtain a unique vector \(y^{\prime} \in \mathrm{V}\) such that \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle\); that is. \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, y^{\prime}\right\rangle\) for all \(r \in \mathrm{~V}\). Defining \(\mathrm{T}^{*}: \mathrm{V} \longrightarrow \mathrm{V}\) by \(\mathrm{T}^{*}(y)=y^{\prime}\), we have \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle\).

    To show that \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) is lincar, let \(y_{1}, y_{2} \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(c \in F\). Then for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). we have

    \[ \begin{aligned} \left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}\left(c y_{1}+y_{2}\right)\right\rangle & =\left\langle\mathrm{T}(. r), c y_{1}+y_{2}\right\rangle \\ & =\bar{c}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}(r), y_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle\mathrm{T}(\cdot r), y_{2}\right\rangle \\ & =\bar{c}\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)\right\rangle+\left\langle r, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)\right\rangle \\ & =\left\langle x \cdot c \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)\right\rangle . \end{aligned} \]

    Since \(x\) is arbitrary: \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(r y_{1}+y_{2}\right)=c \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(y_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(y_{2}\right)\) by Theorem 6.1(e) (p. 333).

    Finally, we need to show that \(T^{*}\) is unique. Suppose that \(U: V \rightarrow V\) is linear and that it satisfics \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x) . y\rangle=\langle r, \mathrm{U}(y)\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Then \(\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle=\langle x, \mathrm{U}(y)\rangle\) for all \(r, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), so \(\mathrm{T}^{*}=\mathrm{U}\).

    The linear operator \(T^{*}\) deseribed in Theorem 6.9 is called the adjoint of the operator \(T\). The symbol \(T\) " is read " \(T\) star."

    Thus \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) is the mique operator on V satisfying \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle r, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle\) for all \(. r, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Note that we also have

    \[ \langle r, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\overline{\langle\mathrm{T}(y), x\rangle}=\overline{\left\langle y, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(x)\right\rangle}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(. x), y\right\rangle ; \]

    so \(\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x), y\right\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). We may view these equations symbolically as adding a \(*\) to \(T\) when shifting its position inside the inner product symbol.

    For an infinite-dimensional inner product space, the adjoint of a lincar operator T may be defined to be the function \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) such that \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x . \mathrm{T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), provided it exists. Although the uniqueness and linearity of \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) follow as before. the existence of the adjoint is not guaranteed (see Exercise 2.4). The reader should ohserve the necessity of the hypothesis of finitedimonsionality in the proof of Theorem 6.8. Many of the theorems we prove about adjoints, nevertheless, do not depend on \(V\) being finite-dimensional. Thus, unless stated otherwiss, for the remainuler of this chapter we adopl the convention that a reference to the adjoint of a linear operator on an infinitedimensional inner product spare assumes its pxistence.

    Theorem 6.10 is a useful result for computing aljoints. Theorem 6.10. Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space. and let 3 be an orthonormal hasis for V . If T is a linear operator on V , then

    \[ \left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{B}=[\mathrm{T}]_{i j}^{*} . \]

    Proof. Let \(A=[T]_{i \beta}, B=\left[T^{*}\right]_{3 i}\), and \(; 3=\left\{v_{1}, r_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\). Then from the corollary to Theorem 6.5) (p. 346), we have

    \[ B_{i j}=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(v_{j}\right), v_{i}\right\rangle=\overline{\left\langle v_{i}, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle}=\overline{\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle}=\overline{\Lambda_{j i}}=\left(A^{*}\right)_{i j} \]

    Hence \(B=A^{*}\). Corollary. Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) matrix. Then \(L_{A^{-}}=\left(L_{A}\right)^{*}\). Proof. If 3 is the standard ordered basis for \(\mathrm{F}^{\prime \prime}\), then, by Theorem \(2.1 \%\) (p. 93), we have \(\left[\mathrm{L}_{A}\right]_{3}=A\). Hence \(\left[\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}\right]_{; 3}=\left[\mathrm{L}_{A}\right]_{;}^{*}=A^{*}=\left[\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right]_{3}\). and so \(\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\).

    As an illustration of Theorem ( 6.10 , we compute the arljoint of arspecific: linear operator.

    Example 2¶

    Let T be the linear operator on \(\mathrm{C}^{-2}\) defined by \(\mathrm{T}\left(a_{1} \cdot a_{2}\right)=\left(2 i a_{1}+3 a_{2}, a_{1}-a_{2}\right)\). If 3 is the standard ordered basis for \(C^{2}\). them

    \[ [T]_{j \beta}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 2 i & 3 \\ 1 & -1 \end{array}\right) . \]

    So

    \[ \left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{i}=[\mathrm{T}]_{i}^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} -2 i & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{array}\right) . \]

    Hence

    \[ \mathrm{T}^{*}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=\left(-2 i a_{1}+a_{2}, 3 a_{1}-a_{2}\right) \]

    The following theorem suggests an analogy between the conjugates of complex numbers and the adjoints of linear operators.

    Theorem 6.11. Let V be an inner product space, and let T and U be linear operators on V . Then (a) \((\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}\); (b) \((c \mathrm{~T})^{*}=\bar{c} \mathrm{~T}^{*}\) for any \(c \in F\); (c) \((\mathrm{TU})^{*}=\mathrm{U}^{*} \mathrm{~T}^{*}\); (d) \(\mathrm{T}^{* *}=\mathrm{T}\); (e) \(I^{*}=I\).

    Proof. We prove (a) and (d); the rest are proved similarly. Let \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). (a) Because

    \[ \begin{aligned} \left\langle x,(\mathrm{~T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}(y)\right\rangle & =\langle(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})(x), y\rangle=\langle\mathrm{T}(x)+\mathrm{U}(x), y\rangle \\ & =\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle+\langle\mathrm{U}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle+\left\langle x, \mathrm{U}^{*}(y)\right\rangle \\ & =\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)+\mathrm{U}^{*}(y)\right\rangle=\left\langle x,\left(\mathrm{~T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}\right)(y)\right\rangle . \end{aligned} \]

    \(\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}\) has the property unique to \((\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}\). Hence \(\mathrm{T}^{*}+\mathrm{U}^{*}=(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U})^{*}\). (d) Similarly, since

    \[ \langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x) \cdot y\right\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{* *}(y)\right\rangle, \]

    (d) follows.

    The same proof works in the infinite-dimensional case, provided that the existence of \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) and \(\mathrm{U}^{*}\) is assumed.

    Coróllary. Let \(A\) and \(B\) be \(n \times n\) matrices. Then (a) \((A+B)^{*}=A^{*}+B^{*}\); (b) \((c A)^{*}=\bar{c} A^{*}\) for all \(c \in F\); (c) \((A B)^{*}=B^{*} A^{*}\); (d) \(A^{* *}=A\); (c) \(I^{*}=I\).

    Proof. We prove only (c): the remaining parts can be proved similarly. Since \(\mathrm{L}_{(A B)^{*}}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{A B}\right)^{*}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{A} \mathrm{~L}_{B}\right)^{*}=\left(\mathrm{L}_{B}\right)^{*}\left(\mathrm{~L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{B^{*}} \mathrm{~L}_{A^{*}}=\mathrm{L}_{B^{*} A^{*}}\), we have \((A B)^{*}=B^{*} A^{*}\).

    In the preceding proof, we relied on the corollary to Theorem 6.10. An alternative proof, which holds even for nonsquare matrices, can be given by appealing directly to the definition of the conjugate transpose of a matrix (see Excrcise 5).

    Least Squares Approximation¶

    Consider the following problem: An experimenter collects data by taking measurencuts \(y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{m}\) at times \(t_{1}, t_{2}, \ldots, t_{m}\), respectively. For example, he or she may be measuring unemployment at various times during some period. Suppose that the data \(\left(t_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left\langle t_{2}, y_{2}\right), \ldots,\left(t_{m}, y_{m}\right)\) are plotted as points in the plane. (See Figure 6.3.) From this plot, the experimenter feels that there exists an essentially linear relationship between \(y\) and \(t\), say \(y=c t+d\), and would like to find the constants \(c\) and \(d\) so that the line \(y=c t+d\) represents the best possible fit to the data collected. One such estimate of fit is to calculate the error \(E\) that represents the sum of the squares of the vertical distances from the points to the line; that is,

    Figure 6.3 Thus the problem is reduced to finding the constants \(c\) and \(d\) that minimize \(E\). (For this reason the line \(y=c t+d\) is called the least squares line.) If we let

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} t_{1} & 1 \\ t_{2} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ t_{m} & 1 \end{array}\right), \quad x=\binom{c}{d}, \quad \text { and } \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{c} y_{1} \\ y_{2} \\ \vdots \\ y_{m} \end{array}\right) \]

    then it follows that \(E=\|y-A x\|^{2}\). We develop a general method for finding an explicit vector \(x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\) that minimizes \(E\); that is, given an \(m \times n\) matrix \(A\), we find \(x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\) such that \(\left\|y-A x_{0}\right\| \leq\|y-A x\|\) for all vectors \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\). This method not only allows us to find the linear function that best fits the data, but also, for any positive integer \(k\), the best fit using a polynomial of degree at most \(k\).

    First, we need some notation and two simple lemmas. For \(x, y \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\), let \(\langle x, y\rangle_{n}\) denote the standard inmer product of \(x\) and \(y\) in \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\). Recall that if \(x\) and \(y\) are regarled as column vectors, then \(\langle x, y\rangle_{n}=y^{*} x\).

    Lemma 1. Let \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F), x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\), and \(y \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\). Then

    \[ \langle A x, y\rangle_{m}=\left\langle x, A^{*} y\right\rangle_{n} \]

    Proof. By a generalization of the corollary to Theorem 6.11 (see Exercise \(5(\mathrm{~b}))\). we have

    \[ \langle A x, y\rangle_{m}=y^{*}(A x)=\left(y^{*} A\right) \cdot x=\left(A^{*} y\right)^{*} \cdot x=\left\langle x \cdot A^{*} y\right\rangle_{n} \]

    Lemma 2. Let \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)\). Then \(\operatorname{rank}\left(A^{*} A\right)=\operatorname{rank}(A)\).

    Proof. By the dimension theorem. we nerd only show that, for \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\), we have \(A^{*} A x=0\) if and only if \(A x=0\). (loarly. \(A x=0\) implies that \(A^{*} A x=0\). So assume that \(A^{*} A x=0\). Then

    \[ 0=\left\langle A^{*} A r, x\right\rangle_{n}=\left\langle A x, A^{* *} \cdot r\right\rangle_{m}=\langle A x, A x\rangle_{m} \]

    so that \(A x=0\). Corollary. If \(A\) is an \(m \times n\) matrix such that \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=n\). then \(A^{*} A\) is invertible.

    Now let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix and \(y \in \mathrm{~F}^{\prime \prime}\). Dodine \(\mathrm{W}=\left\{A x: x \in \mathrm{~F}^{\prime}\right\}\) : that is, \(W=R\left(L_{A}\right)\). By the corollary to Theorem 6.6 ( p .350 ), there exists a unique vector in \(W\) that is closest to \(y\). Call this vector \(A x_{0}\), where \(r_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\). Then \(\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\| \leq\|A x-y\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\); so .rol has the property that \(E=\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\|\) is minimal, as desired.

    To develop a practical method for finding such an \(r_{0}\), we note from The- all \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\). Thus, by Lemma 1, we have that \(\left\langle x, A^{*}\left(A r_{0}-y\right)\right\rangle_{n}=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\); that is, \(A^{*}\left(A x_{0}-y\right)=0\). So we need only find a solution \(x_{0}\) to \(A^{*} A x=A^{*} y\). If, in addition, we assume that \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=n\), then by Lemma 2 we have \(x_{0}=\left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1} A^{*} y\). We summarize this discussion in the following theorem.

    Theorem 6.12. Let \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\). Then there exists \(x_{0} \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\) such that \(\left(A^{*} A\right) x_{0}=A^{*} y\) and \(\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\| \leq\|A . r-y\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\). Furthermore, if \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=n\), then \(x_{0}=\left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1} A^{*} y\).

    To return to our experimenter, let us suppose that the data collected are \((1,2),(2,3),(3.5)\), and \((4,7)\). Then

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{array}\right) \]

    hence

    \[ A^{*} A=\left(\begin{array}{llll} 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 30 & 10 \\ 10 & 4 \end{array}\right) \]

    Thus

    \[ \left(A^{*} A\right)^{-1}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -10 \\ -10 & 30 \end{array}\right) . \]

    Therefore

    \[ \binom{c}{d}=x_{0}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -10 \\ -10 & 30 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{llll} 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{array}\right)=\binom{1.7}{0} . \]

    It follows that the line \(y=1.71\) is the least spuares line. The error \(E\) paty be computed directly as \(\left\|A x_{0}-y\right\|^{2}=0.3\).

    Suppose that the experimenter chose the times \(t_{i}(1 \leq i \leq m)\) to satisfy

    \[ \sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i}=0 \]

    Then the two columes of \(A\) wond be orthogonal. so \(A^{*} A\) would be a diagonal matrix (see Exercise 19). In this case, the computations are greatly simplified.

    In practice, the \(m \times 2\) matrix \(A\) in our least squares application has rank equal to two, and hence \(A^{*} A\) is invertible by the corollary to Lemmat 2 . For, otherwise, the first column of \(A\) is a multiple of the second column. which consists only of ones. But this wonld occur only if the experimenter collects all the data at exactly one time.

    Finally, the method above may also be applied if, for some \(k\), the experimenter wants to fit a polynomial of degree at most \(k\) to the data. ' For instance. if a polynomial \(y=c t^{2}+d t+e\) of degree at most 2 is desired, the appropriate model is

    \[ r=\left(\begin{array}{c} r \\ d \\ r \end{array}\right), \quad y=\left(\begin{array}{c} y_{1} \\ y_{2} \\ \vdots \\ y_{m} \end{array}\right), \quad \text { andl } \quad A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} t_{1}^{2} & t_{1} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ l_{m}^{2} & t_{m} & 1 \end{array}\right) . \]

    Minimal Solutions to Systems of Linear Equations¶

    Even when a system of linear equations \(A x=b\) is consistent, there may be no unique solution. In such cases, it may be desirable to find a solution of minimal norm. A solution \(s\) to \(A x=b\) is called a minimal solution if \(\|s\| \leq\) \(\|u\|\) for all other solutions \(u\). The next theorem assures that every consistent system of linear equations has a unique minimal solution and provides a method for computing it.

    Theorem 6.13. Let \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{m \times n}(F)\) and \(b \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\). Suppose that \(A x=b\) is consistent. Then the following statements are true. (a) There exists exactly one minimal solution \(s\) of \(A x=b\), and \(s \in \mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\right)\). (b) The vector \(s\) is the only solution to \(A x=b\) that lies in \(\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{L}_{A} \cdot\right)\); that is, if \(u\) satisfices \(\left(A A^{*}\right) u=b\), then \(s=A^{*} u\).

    Proof. (a) For simplicity of notation, we let \(W=R\left(L_{A^{*}}\right)\) and \(W^{\prime}=N\left(L_{A}\right)\). Let \(x\) be any solution to \(A x=b\). By Theorem 6.6 (p. 350), \(x=s+y\) for some \(s \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\). But \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}^{\prime}\) by Exercise 12, and therefore \(b=A x=A s+A y=A s\). So \(s\) is a solution to \(A x=b\) that lies in W. To prove (a), we need only show that \(s\) is the unique minimal solution. Let \(v\) be any solution to \(A x=b\). By Theorem 3.9 (p. 172), we have that \(v=s+u\), where \(u \in \mathrm{~W}^{\prime}\). Since \(s \in \mathrm{~W}\), which equals \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\) by Exercise 12, we have

    \[ \because \quad\|v\|^{2}=\|s+u\|^{2}=\|s\|^{2}+\|u\|^{2} \geq\|s\|^{2} \]

    by Exercise 10 of Section 6.1. Thus \(s\) is a minimal solution. We can also see from the preceding calculation that if \(\|v\|=\|s\|\), then \(u=0\); hence \(v=s\). Therefore \(s\) is the unique minimal solution to \(A x=b\), proving (a). (b) Assume that \(v\) is also a solution to \(A x=b\) that lies in W . Then

    \[ v-s \in W \cap W^{\prime}=W \cap W^{\perp}=\{0\} ; \]

    so \(v=s\). Finally, suppose that \(\left(A A^{*}\right) u=b\). and let \(v=A^{*} u\). Then \(v \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(A v=b\). Therefore \(s=v=A^{*} u\) by the discussion above.

    Example 3¶

    Consider the system

    \[ \begin{aligned} x+2 y+z & =4 \\ x-y+2 z & =-11 \\ x+5 y & =19 . \end{aligned} \]

    Let

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad b=\left(\begin{array}{r} 4 \\ -11 \\ 19 \end{array}\right) \]

    To find the minimal solution to this system, we must first find some solution \(u\) to \(A A^{*} x=b\). Now

    \[ A A^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 6 & 1 & 11 \\ 1 & 6 & -4 \\ 11 & -4 & 26 \end{array}\right) ; \]

    so we consider the system

    \[ \begin{aligned} 6 x+y+11 z= & 4 \\ x+6 y-4 z= & -11 \\ 11 x-4 y+26 z= & 19 \end{aligned} \]

    for which one solution is

    \[ u=\left(\begin{array}{r} 1 \\ -2 \\ 0 \end{array}\right) \]

    (Any solution will suffice.) Hence

    \[ s=A^{*} u=\left(\begin{array}{r} -1 \\ 4 \\ -3 \end{array}\right) \]

    is the minimal solution to the given system.

    EXERCISES¶

    1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying imer product spaces are finite-dimensional. (a) Every lincar operator has an adjoint. (b) Every lincar operator on \(V\) has the form \(x \rightarrow\langle x, y\rangle\) for some \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\). (c) For every lincar operator T on V and every ordered basis 3 for V , we have \(\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{a}=\left([\mathrm{T}]_{i}\right)^{*}\). (d) The adjoint of a linear operator is mique. (e) For any lincor operators T and U and scalars \(a\) and \(b\),
    \[ (a \mathrm{~T}+b \mathrm{U})^{*}=a \mathrm{~T}^{*}+b \mathrm{U}^{*} \]

    (f) For any \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\). we lave \(\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{L}_{A^{*}}\). (g) For any linear operator T , we have \(\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)^{*}=\mathrm{T}\). 2. For each of the following inner prochuct spaces \(V\) (over \(F\) ) and linear transformations \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\), find a vector \(y\) surl that \(\mathrm{g}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{3}, \mathrm{~g}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right)=a_{1}-2 a_{2}+4 a_{3}\) (b) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{2}, \mathrm{~g}\left(\tilde{\sim}_{1}, z_{2}\right)=z_{1}-2 z_{2}\) (c) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) with \(\langle f, h\rangle=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{l}} f(t) h(t) d t, \mathrm{~g}(f)=f(0)+f^{\prime}(1)\) 3. For each of the following inner product spaces \(V\) and linear operators \(T\) on \(V\), evaluate \(T^{*}\) at the given vector in \(V\). (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{2}, \mathrm{~T}(a, b)=(2 a+b, a-3 b), x=(3,5)\). (b) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{2}, \mathrm{~T}\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)=\left(2 z_{1}+i z_{2},(1-i) z_{1}\right), x=(3-i .1+2 i)\). (c) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) with \(\langle f: g\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t . \mathrm{T}(f)=f^{\prime}+3 f\), \(f(t)=4-2 t\) 4. Complete the proof of Theorem 6.11. 5. (a) Complete the proof of the corollary to Theorem 6.11 by using Theorem 6.11, as in the proof of (c). (b) State a result for nonsquare matrices that is analogous to the corollary to Theorem 6.11, and prove it using a matrix argument. 6. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on an inner product space \(V\). Let \(U_{1}=T+T^{*}\) and \(U_{2}=T T^{*}\). Prove that \(U_{1}=U_{1}^{*}\) and \(U_{2}=U_{2}^{*}\). 7. Give an example of a lincar operator \(T\) on an inner product space \(V\) such that \(N(T) \neq N\left(T^{*}\right)\). 8. Let V be a finite-dimensional imer product space. and let T be a linear operator on \(V\). Prove that if \(T\) is invertible, then \(T^{*}\) is invertible and \(\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)^{-1}=\left(\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right)^{*}\). 9. Prove that if \(V=W \oplus W^{\perp}\) and \(T\) is the projection on \(W\) along \(W^{\perp}\). then \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\). IIfint: Recall that \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})=\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\). (For definitions, sce the exercises of Sections 1.3 and 2.1.) 10. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on an inner product space \(V\). Prove that \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) if and only if \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Hint: Use Exercise 20 of Section 6.1. 11. For a linear operator \(T\) on an inner product space \(V\), prove that \(T^{*} T=\) \(T_{0}\) implies \(T=T_{0}\). Is the same result true if we assume that \(T^{*}=T_{0}\) ? 12. Let V be an imer product space, and let T be a lincar operator on V . Prove the following results. (a) \(\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)^{\perp}=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\). (b) If \(V\) is finite-dimensional, then \(R\left(T^{*}\right)=N(T)^{\perp}\). Hint: Use Exercise 13(c) of Section 6.2. 13. Let \(T\) be a lincar operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\). Prove the following results. (a) \(N\left(T^{*} T\right)=N(T)\). Deduce that \(\operatorname{rank}\left(T^{*} T\right)=\operatorname{rank}(T)\). (b) \(\operatorname{rank}(T)=\operatorname{rank}\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)\). Deduce from (a) that \(\operatorname{rank}\left(\mathrm{TT}^{*}\right)=\operatorname{rank}(\mathrm{T})\). (c) For any \(n \times n\) matrix \(A, \operatorname{rank}\left(A^{*} A\right)=\operatorname{rank}\left(A A^{*}\right)=\operatorname{rank}(A)\). 14. Let V be an inner product space, and let \(y, z \in \mathrm{~V}\). Define \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) by \(\mathrm{T}(x)=\langle x, y\rangle z\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). First prove that T is linear. Then show that \(T^{*}\) exists, and find an explicit expression for it.

    The following definition is used in Exercises 15-17 and is an extension of the definition of the adjoint of a lincar operator.

    Definition. Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation, where V and W are finite-dimensional inner product spaces with inner products \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{1}\) and \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{2}\), respectively. A function \(\mathrm{T}^{*}: \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) is called an adjoint of T if \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle_{2}=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle_{1}\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~W}\). 15. Let \(T: V \rightarrow W\) be a linear transformation, where \(V\) and \(W\) are finitedimensional inner product spaces with inner products \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{1}\) and \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle_{2}\), respectively. Prove the following results. (a) There is a unique adjoint \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) of T , and \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) is linear. (b) If \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) are orthonormal bases for V and W , respectively, then \(\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{\gamma}^{\beta}=\left([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{\gamma}\right)^{*}\). (c) \(\operatorname{rank}\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}\right)=\operatorname{rank}(\mathrm{T})\). (d) \(\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x), y\right\rangle_{1}=\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle_{2}\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\). (e) For all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{~T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}(x)=0\) if and only if \(\mathrm{T}(x)=0\). 16. State and prove a result that extends the first four parts of Theorem 6.11 using the preceding definition. 17. Let \(T: V \rightarrow W\) be a linear transformation, where \(V\) and \(W\) are finitedimensional inner product spaces. Prove that \(\left(R\left(T^{*}\right)\right)^{\perp}=N(T)\), using the preceding definition. 18. \({ }^{\dagger}\) Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) matrix. Prove that \(\operatorname{det}\left(A^{*}\right)=\overline{\operatorname{det}(A)}\). 19. Suppose that \(A\) is an \(m \times n\) matrix in which no two columns are identical. Prove that \(A^{*} A\) is a diagonal matrix if and only if every pair of columns of \(A\) is orthogonal. 20. For each of the sets of data that follows, use the least squares approximation to find the best fits with both (i) a linear function and (ii) a quadratic function. Compute the error \(E\) in both cases. (a) \(\{(-3,9),(-2,6),(0,2),(1,1)\}\) (b) \(\{(1,2),(3,4),(5,7),(7,9),(9,12)\}\) (c) \(\{(-2,4),(-1,3),(0,1),(1,-1),(2,-3)\}\) 21. In physics, Hooke's law states that (within certain limits) there is a linear relationship between the length \(x\) of a spring and the force \(y\) applied to (or exerted by) the spring. That is, \(y=c x+d\), where \(c\) is called the spring constant. Use the following data to estimate the spring constant (the length is given in inches and the force is given in pounds).

    Length Force
    \(x\) \(y\)
    3.5 1.0
    4.0 2.2
    4.5 2.8
    5.0 4.3
    1. Find the minimal solution to cach of the following systems of linear equations. (a) \(x+2 y-z=12\)
    \[ x+2 y-z=1 \]

    (b) \(2 x+3 y+z=2\)

    \[ 4 x+7 y-z=4 \]

    (c) \(\begin{aligned} x+y-z & =0 \\ 2 x-y+z & =3 \\ x-y+z & =2\end{aligned}\) (d) \(\begin{aligned} x+y+z-w & =1 \\ 2 x-y+w & =1\end{aligned}\) 23. Consider the problem of finding the least squares line \(y=c t+d\) corresponding to the \(m\) obscrvations \(\left(t_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(t_{2}, y_{2}\right), \ldots,\left(t_{m}, y_{m}\right)\). (a) Show that the equation \(\left(A^{*} A\right) x_{0}=A^{*} y\) of Theorem 6.12 takes the form of the normal equations:

    \[ \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i}^{2}\right) c+\left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i}\right) d=\sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i} y_{i} \]

    and

    \[ \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i}\right) c+m d=\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_{i} \]

    These equations may also be obtained from the error \(E\) by setting the partial derivatives of \(E\) with respect to both \(c\) and \(d\) equal to zero. (b) Use the second normal equation of (a) to show that the least squares line must pass through the center of mass, \((\bar{t}, \bar{y})\), where

    \[ \vec{t}=\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} t_{i} \quad \text { and } \quad \bar{y}=\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_{i} . \]
    1. Let \(V\) and \(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots\right\}\) be defined as in Exercise 23 of Section 6.2. Define \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) by
    \[ \mathrm{T}(\sigma)(k)=\sum_{i=k}^{\infty} \sigma(i) \quad \text { for cevery positive integer } k \]

    Notice that the infinite serics in the definition of \(T\) converges because \(\sigma(i) \neq 0\) for only finitcly many \(i\). (a) Prove that T is a linear operator on V . (b) Prove that for any positive integer \(n, \mathrm{~T}\left(e_{n}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_{i}\). (c) Prove that \(T\) has no adjoint. Hint: By way of contradiction, suppose that \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) exists. Prove that for any positive integer \(n\), \(T^{*}\left(e_{n}\right)(k) \neq 0\) for infinitely many \(k\).

    6.4 NORMAL AND SELF-ADJOINT OPERATORS¶

    We have seen the importance of diagonalizable operators in Chapter 5. For these operators, it is necessary and sufficient for the vector space \(V\) to possess a basis of eigenvectors. As \(V\) is an inner product space in this chapter, it is reasonable to seek conditions that guarantee that V has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors. A very important result that helps achieve our goal is Schur's theorem (Theorem 6.14). The formulation that follows is in terms of linear operators. The next section contains the more firmiliar matrix form. We begin with a lemma.

    Lemma. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\). If \(T\) has an eigenvector, then so does \(T^{*}\).

    Proof. Suppose that \(v\) is an cigenvector of T with corresponding eigenvalue \(\lambda\). Then for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\),

    \[ 0=\langle 0, x\rangle=\langle(\mathrm{T}-\lambda \mathrm{I})(v), x\rangle=\left\langle v,(\mathrm{~T}-\lambda I)^{*}(x)\right\rangle=\left\langle v,\left(\mathrm{~T}^{*}-\bar{\lambda}\right)(x)\right\rangle, \]

    and hence \(v\) is orthogonal to the range of \(T^{*}-\bar{\lambda}\). So \(T^{*}-\bar{\lambda} l\) is not onto and hence is not one-to-one. Thus \(\mathrm{T}^{*}-\bar{\lambda} l\) has a nonzero null space, and any nonzero vector in this null space is an eigenvector of \(T^{*}\) with corresponding eigenvalue \(\bar{\lambda}\).

    Recall (see the exercises of Section 2.1 and see Section 5.4) that a subspace \(W\) of \(V\) is said to be \(T\)-invariant if \(T(W)\) is contained in \(W\). If \(W\) is \(T\). invariant, we may define the restriction \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{W}}: \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) by \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{W}}(x)=\mathrm{T}(x)\) for all \(x \in W\). It is clear that \(T_{W}\) is a lincar operator on W. Recall from Section 5.2 that a polynomial is said to split if it factors into linear polynomials.

    Theorem 6.14 (Schur). Let \(T\) be a linear operator on a finitedimensional inuer product space V. Suppose that the characteristic polynomial of T splits. Then there exists an orthonornal basis \(\beta\) for V such that the matrix \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\) is upper triangular.

    Proof. The proof is by mathematical induction on the dimension \(n\) of \(V\). The result is immediate if \(n=1\). So suppose that the result is true for linear operators on ( \(n-1\) )-dimensional imer product spaces whose characteristic polynomials split. By the lemma, we can assume that \(T^{*}\) has a unit eigenvector \(z\). Suppose that \(T^{*}(z)=\lambda z\) and that \(W=\operatorname{span}(\{z\})\). We show that \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\) is T -invariant. If \(y \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\) and \(x=c z \in \mathrm{~W}\), then

    \[ \begin{aligned} \langle\mathrm{T}(y): x\rangle & =\langle\mathrm{T}(y), c z\rangle=\left\langle y, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(c z)\right\rangle=\left\langle y, c \mathrm{~T}^{*}(z)\right\rangle=\langle y, c \lambda z\rangle \\ & =\overline{c \lambda}\langle y, z\rangle=\overline{c \cdot}(0)=0 . \end{aligned} \]

    So \(T(y) \in W^{\perp}\). It is easy to show (see Theorem 5.21p. 314, or as a consequence of Exercise 6 of Section 4.4) that the characteristic polynomial of \(T_{W}\) d divides the characteristic polynomial of \(T\) and hence splits. By Theorem 6.7(c) (p. 352), \(\operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\right)=n-1\), so we may apply the induction hypothesis to \(T_{W}\) and obtain an orthonormal basis \(\gamma\) of \(W^{\perp}\) such that \(\left[T_{W_{\perp}}\right]_{\gamma}\) is upper triangular. Clearly, \(\beta=\gamma \cup\{z\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(V\) such that \([T]_{\beta}\) is upper triangular.

    We now return to our original goal of finding an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of a linear operator \(T\) on a finite-dimensional imer product space V. Note that if such an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) exists, then \([T]_{\beta}\) is a diagonal matrix, and hence \(\left[T^{*}\right]_{\beta}=[T]_{3}^{*}\) is also a diagonal matrix. Because diagonal matrices commute. we conclude that T and \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) commute. Thus if V possesses an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of T , then \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\).

    Definitions. Let \(V\) be an inner product space, and let \(T\) be a linear operator on V . We say that T is normal if \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\). An \(n \times n\) real or complex matrix \(A\) is normal if \(A A^{*}=A^{*} A\).

    It follows immediately from Theorem 6.10 (p. 359) that \(T\) is normal if and only if \([T]_{\beta}\) is normal, where \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis.

    Example 1¶

    Let \(T: R^{2} \rightarrow R^{2}\) be rotation by \(\theta\), where \(0<0<\pi\). The matrix representation of \(T\) in the standard ordered basis is given by

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right) \]

    Note that \(A A^{*}=I=A^{*} A\); so \(A\), and hence T . is normal.

    Example 2¶

    Suppose that \(A\) is a real skew-symmetric matrix; that is, \(\Lambda^{t}=-\Lambda\). Then \(A\) is normal because both \(A A^{t}\) and \(A^{t} A\) are equal to \(-A^{2}\).

    Clearly, the operator T in Example 1 docs not even possess one eigenvector. So in the case of a real inner product space, we see that normality is not sufficient to guarantec an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors. All is not lost, however. We show that normality suffices if V is a complex inner product space.

    Before we prove the promised result for normal operators, we need some general properties of normal operators.

    Theorem 6.15. Let \(V\) be an inner product space, and let T be a normal operator on V . Then the following statements are truc. (a) \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\left\|\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)\right\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). (b) \(T-c l\) is normal for every \(c \in F\). (c) If \(x\) is an eigenvector of \(T\), then \(x\) is also an eigenvector of \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\). In fact, if \(\mathrm{T}(x)=\lambda x\), then \(\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)=\bar{\lambda} x\). (d) If \(\lambda_{1}\) and \(\lambda_{2}\) are distinct cigenvalucs of \(T\) with corresponding cigenvectors \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\). thon \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\) are orthogonal.

    Proof. (a) For any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2} & =\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(x)\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}(x), x\right\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T} \mathrm{~T}^{*}(x) ; x\right\rangle \\ & =\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(x), \mathrm{T}^{*}(x)\right\rangle=\left\|\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)\right\|^{2} . \end{aligned} \]

    The proof of (b) is left as an exercise. (c) Suppose that \(\mathrm{T}(x)=\lambda x\) for some \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Let \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T}-\lambda \mathrm{l}\). Then \(\mathrm{U}(x)=0\), and U is normal by (b). Thus (a) implies that

    \[ 0=\|\mathrm{U}(x)\|=\left\|\mathrm{U}^{*}(x)\right\|=\left\|\left(\mathrm{T}^{*}-\bar{\lambda}\right)(x)\right\|=\left\|\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)-\bar{\lambda} x\right\| . \]

    Hence \(\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)=\bar{\lambda} x\). So \(x\) is an cigenvector of \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\). (d) Let \(\lambda_{1}\) and \(\lambda_{2}\) be distinct eigenvalues of \(T\) with corresponding eigenvectors \(x_{1}\) and \(x_{2}\). Then, using (c), we have

    \[ \lambda_{1}\left\langle x_{1}, x_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle\lambda_{1} x_{1}, x_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}\right), x_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(x_{2}\right)\right\rangle \]
    \[ =\left\langle x_{1}, \overline{\lambda_{2}} x_{2}\right\rangle=\lambda_{2}\left\langle x_{1}, \dot{x}_{2}\right\rangle . \]

    Since \(\lambda_{1} \neq \lambda_{2}\), we conclude that \(\left\langle x_{1}, x_{2}\right\rangle=0\). Theorem 6.16. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space V . Then T is normal if and only if there exists an orthonormal basis for V consisting of eigenvectors of T .

    Proof. Suppose that \(T\) is normal. By the fundamental theorem of algebra (Theorem D.4), the characteristic polynomial of T splits. So we may apply Schur's theorem to obtain an orthonormal basis \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V such that \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}=A\) is upper triangular. We know that \(v_{1}\) is an eigenvector of \(T\) because \(A\) is upper triangular. Assume that \(v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k-1}\) are eigenvectors of T . We claim that \(v_{k}\) is also an eigenvector of T . It then follows by mathematical induction on \(k\) that all of the \(v_{i}\) 's are eigenvectors of T . Consider any \(j<k\), and let \(\lambda_{j}\) denote the cigenvalue of \(T\) corresponding to \(v_{j}\). By Theorem 6.15, \(T^{*}\left(v_{j}\right)=\bar{\lambda}_{j} v_{j}\). Since, \(A\) is upper triangular,

    \[ \mathrm{T}\left(v_{k}\right)=A_{1 k} v_{1}+A_{2 k} v_{2}+\cdots+A_{j k} v_{j}+\cdots+A_{k k} v_{k} \]

    Furthermore, by the corollary to Theorem 6.5 (p. 347),

    \[ A_{j k}=\left\langle\mathbf{T}\left(v_{k}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{k}, \mathbf{T}^{*}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{k}, \bar{\lambda}_{j} v_{j}\right\rangle=\lambda_{j}\left\langle v_{k}, v_{j}\right\rangle=0 . \]

    It follows that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{k}\right)=A_{k k} v_{k}\), and hence \(v_{k}\) is an eigenvector of T. So by induction, all the vectors in \(\beta\) are eigenvectors of T .

    The converse was already proved on page 370 . Intercstingly, as the next example shows, Theorem 6.16 does not extend to infinite-dimensional complex inner product spaces.

    Example 3¶

    Consider the inner product space H with the orthonormal set \(S\) from Example 9 in Section 6.1. Let \(V=\operatorname{span}(S)\), and let \(T\) and \(U\) be the lincar operators on V defined by \(\mathrm{T}(f)=f_{1} f\) and \(\mathrm{U}(f)=f_{-1} f\). Then

    \[ \mathrm{T}\left(f_{n}\right)=f_{n+1} \quad \text { and } \quad \mathrm{U}\left(f_{n}\right)=f_{n-1} \]

    for all integers \(n\). Thus

    \[ \left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(f_{m}\right), f_{n}\right\rangle=\left\langle f_{m+1}, f_{n}\right\rangle=\delta_{(m+1), n}=\delta_{m,(n-1)}=\left\langle f_{m}, f_{n-1}\right\rangle=\left\langle f_{m}, \mathrm{U}\left(f_{n}\right)\right\rangle \]

    It follows that \(U=T^{*}\). Furthermore, \(T T^{*}=I=T^{*} T\); so \(T\) is normal. We show that \(T\) has no eigenvectors. Suppose that \(f\) is an cigenvector of T, say, \(\mathrm{T}(f)=\lambda f\) for some \(\lambda\). Since. \(V\) equals the span of \(S\), we may write

    \[ f=\sum_{i=n}^{m} a_{i} f_{i}, \quad \text { where } a_{m} \neq 0 \]

    Hence

    \[ \sum_{i=n}^{m} a_{i} f_{i+1}=\mathrm{T}(f)=\lambda f=\sum_{i=n}^{m} \lambda a_{i} f_{i} . \]

    Since \(a_{m} \neq 0\), we can write \(f_{m+1}\) as a linear combination of \(f_{n}, f_{n+1}, \ldots, f_{m}\). But this is a contradiction because \(S\) is linearly independent.

    Example 1 illustrates that normality is not sufficient to guarantee the existence of an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors for real inner product spaces.
