For real inner product spaces, we must replace normality by the stronger condition that \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\) in order to guarantee such a basis.

    Definitions. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on an inner product space \(V\). We say that T is self-adjoint (Hermitian) if \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\). An \(n \times n\) real or complex matrix \(A\) is self-adjoint (Hermitian) if \(A=A^{*}\).

    It follows immediately that if \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis, then T is selfadjoint if and only if \([T]_{\beta}\) is self-adjoint. For real matrices, this condition reduces to the requirement that \(A\) be symmetric.

    Before we state our main result for self-adjoint operators, we need some preliminary work.

    By definition, a linear operator on a real inner product space has only real cigenvalues. The lemma that follows shows that the same can the said for self-adjoint operators on a complex inner product space. Similarly, the characteristic polynomial of every lincar operator on a complex inner product space splits, and the same is true for self-adjoint operators on a real inner product space.

    Lemma. Let \(T\) be a self-adjoint operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V . Then (a) Every cigenvalue of T is real. (b) Suppose that \(V\) is a real inner product space. Then the characteristic polynomial of T splits.

    Proof. (a) Suppose that \(\mathrm{T}(x)=\lambda x\) for \(x \neq 0\). Because a self-adjoint operator is also normal, we can apply Theorem 6.15(c) to obtain

    \[ \lambda x=\mathbf{T}(x)=\mathbf{T}^{*}(x)=\bar{\lambda} x . \]

    So \(\lambda=\bar{\lambda}\); that is, \(\lambda\) is real. (b) Let \(n=\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{V}), \beta\) be an orthonormal basis for V , and \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\). Then \(A\) is self-adjoint. Let \(\mathrm{T}_{A}\) be the linear operator on \(\mathrm{C}^{n}\) defined by. \(\mathrm{T}_{A}(x)=A x\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{C}^{n}\). Note that \(\mathrm{T}_{A}\) is self-adjoint because \(\left[\mathrm{T}_{A}\right]_{\gamma}=A\), where \(\gamma\) is the standard ordered (orthonormal) basis for \(C^{n}\). So, by (a), the cigenvalues of \(T_{A}\) are real. By the fundamental theorem of algebra, the characteristic polynomial of \(T_{A}\) splits into factors of the form \(t-\lambda\). Since cach \(\lambda\) is real, the characteristic polynomial splits over \(R\). But \(\mathrm{T}_{A}\) has the same characteristic polynomial as \(A\), which has the same characteristic polynomial as T . Therefore the characteristic polynomial of T splits.

    We are now able to establish one of the major results of this chapter. Theorem 6.17. Let \(T\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional real inner product space V . Thon T is self-adjoint if and only if there exists an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for \(\vee\) consisting of eigenvectors of T .

    Proof. Suppose that T is self-adjoint. By the lemma, we may apply Schur's theorem to obtain an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for V such that the matrix \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\) is upper triangular. But

    \[ A^{*}=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{*}=\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{\beta}=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}=A \]

    So \(A\) and \(A^{*}\) are both upper triangular, and therefore \(A\) is a diagonal matrix. Thus \(\beta\) must consist of eigenvectors of T .

    The converse is left as an exercise. Theorem 6.17 is used extensively in many areas of mathematics and statistics. We restate this theorem in matrix form in the next section.

    Example 4¶

    As we noted carlier, real symmetric matrices are self-adjoint, and self-adjoint matrices are normal. The following matrix \(A\) is complex and symmetric:

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} i & i \\ i & 1 \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad A^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} -i & -i \\ -i & 1 \end{array}\right) \]

    But \(A\) is not normal, because \(\left(A A^{*}\right)_{12}=1+i\) and \(\left(A^{*} A\right)_{12}=1-i\). Therefore complex symmetric matrices need not be normal.

    EXERCISES¶

    1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying inner product spaces are finite-dimensional. (a) Every self-adjoint operator is normal. (b) Operators and their adjoints have the same eigenvectors. (c) If \(T\) is an operator on an inner product space \(V\), then \(T\) is normal if and only if \([T]_{\beta}\) is normal, where \(\beta\) is any ordered basis for \(V\). (d) A real or complex matrix \(A\) is normal if and only if \(L_{A}\) is normal. (e) The eigenvalucs of a self-adjoint operator must all be real. (f) The identity and zero operators are self-adjoint. (g) Every normal operator is diagonalizable. (h) Every self-adjoint operator is diagonalizable.
    2. For each linear operator \(T\) on an inner product space \(V\), determine whether T is normal, self-adjoint, or neither. If possible, produce an orthonormal basis of cigenvectors of T for V and list the corresponding eigenvalues. (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{2}\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(a, b)=(2 a-2 b,-2 a+5 b)\). (b) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}^{3}\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(a, b, c)=(-a+b, 5 b, 4 a-2 b+5 c)\). (c) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}^{2}\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(a, b)=(2 a+i b, a+2 b)\). (d) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(f)=f^{\prime}\), where
    \[ \langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) g(t) d l \]

    (e) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(R)\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(A)=A^{t}\). (f) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{M}_{2 \times 2}(R)\) and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}\left(\begin{array}{ll}a & b \\ c & d\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ll}c & d \\ a & b\end{array}\right)\). 3. Give an example of a linear operator \(T\) on \(R^{2}\) and an ordered basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) that provides a counterexample to the statement in Exercise 1(c). 4. Let \(T\) and \(U\) be self-adjoint operators on an inner product space \(V\). Prove that TU is self-adjoint if and only if \(T U=U T\). 5. Prove (b) of Theorem 6.15. 6. Let V be a complex inner product space, and let T be a lincar operator on \(V\). Define

    \[ \mathrm{T}_{1}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}+\mathrm{T}^{*}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathrm{T}_{2}=\frac{1}{2 i}\left(\mathrm{~T}-\mathrm{T}^{*}\right) \]

    (a) Prove that \(T_{1}\) and \(T_{2}\) are self-adjoint and that \(T=T_{1}+i T_{2}\). (b) Suppose also that \(T=U_{1}+i \mathrm{U}_{2}\), where \(\mathrm{U}_{1}\) and \(\mathrm{U}_{2}\) are self-adjoint, Prove that \(U_{1}=T_{1}\) and \(U_{2}=T_{2}\). (c) Prove that \(T\) is normal if and only if \(T_{1} T_{2}=T_{2} T_{1}\). 7. Let \(T\) be a lincar operator on an inner product space \(V\), and let \(W\) be a \(T\)-invariant subspace of \(V\). Prove the following results. (a) If \(T\) is self-adjoint, then \(T_{W}\) is self-adjoint. (b) \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\) is \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\)-invàriant. (c) If \(W\) is both \(T\) - and \(T^{*}\)-invariant, then \(\left(T_{W}\right)^{*}=\left(T^{*}\right)_{W}\). (d) If \(W\) is both \(T\) - and \(T^{*}\)-invariant and \(T\) is normal, then \(T_{W}\) is normal. 8. Let \(T\) be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space \(V\), and let \(W\) be a sulspace of \(V\). Prove that if \(W\) is T-invariant, then \(W\) is also \(T^{*}\)-invariant. Hint: Use Excrcise 24 of Section 5.4. 9. Let T be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\). Prove that \(N(T)=N\left(T^{*}\right)\) and \(R(T)=R\left(T^{*}\right)\). Ifint: Use Theorem 6.15 and Exercise 12 of Section 6.3. 10. Let \(T\) be a self-adjoint operator on a finite-dimensional immer product space V . Prove that for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\)

    \[ \|\mathrm{T}(x) \pm i x\|^{2}=\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2}+\|x\|^{2} \]

    Deduce that \(T-i l\) is invertible and that \(\left[(T-i l)^{-1}\right]^{*}=(T+i l)^{-1}\). 11. Assume that \(T\) is a linear operator on a complex (not necessarily finitedimensional) inner product space V with an adjoint. \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\). Prove the following results. (a) If T is self-adjoint, then \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), x\rangle\) is real for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). (b) If T satisfies \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), x\rangle=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), then \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\). Hint: Replace \(x\) by \(x+y\) and then by \(x+i y\), and expand the resulting imucr products. (c) If \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x) \cdot x\rangle\) is real for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), then \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\). 12. Let \(T\) be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional real inner product space \(V\) whose characteristic polynomial splits. Prove that \(V\) has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of \(T\). Hence prove that \(T\) is selfadjoint. 13. An \(n \times n\) real matrix \(A\) is said to be a Gramian matrix if there exists a real (square) matrix \(B\) such that \(A=B^{\dagger} B\). Prove that \(A\) is a Gramian matrix if and only if \(A\) is symmetric and all of its eigenvalucs are nonnegative. Hint: Apply Theorem 6.17 to \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{L}_{A}\) to obtain an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots . v_{n}\right\}\) of eigenvectors with the associated cigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}\). Define the linear operator \(U\) by \(\mathrm{U}\left(v_{i}\right)=\sqrt{\lambda_{i}} v_{i}\). 14. Simultaneous Diagonalization. Let V be a finite-dimensional real inner product space, and let \(U\) and \(T\) be self-adjoint linear operators on \(V\) such that UT \(=\) TU. Prove that there exists an orthonormal basis for V consisting of vectors that are eigenvectors of both U and T . (The complex version of this result appcars as Exercise 10 of Section 6.6.) Hint: For any cigenspace \(W=E_{\lambda}\) of \(T\), we have that \(W\) is both \(T\) - and U-invariant. By Exercise 7, we have that \(W^{\perp}\) is both \(T\) - and \(U\)-invariant. Apply Theorem 6.17 and Theorem 6.6 (p. 350). 15. Let \(A\) and \(B\) be symmetric \(n \times n\) matrices such that \(A B=B A\). Use Exercise 14 to prove that there exists an orthogonal matrix \(P\) such that \(P^{t} A P^{\prime}\) and \(P^{\prime} B P\) are both diagonal matrices. 16. Prove the Cayley-Hamilton theorem for a complex \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\). That is, if \(f(t)\) is the characteristic polynomial of \(A\), prove that \(f(A)=0\). IInt: Use Schur's theorem to show that \(A\) may be assumed to be upper triangular, in which casc

    \[ f(t)=\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(A_{i i}-t\right) \]

    Now if \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{L}_{A}\), we have \(\left(\Lambda_{j j} \mid-\mathrm{T}\right)\left(e_{j}\right) \in \operatorname{span}\left(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots, e_{j-1}\right\}\right)\) for \(j \geq 2\), where \(\left\{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots, e_{n}\right\}\) is the standard ordered basis for \(C^{\prime \prime}\). (The general case is proved in Scetion 5.4.)

    The following definitions are used in Exercises 17 through 23. Definitions. A linear operator T on a finite-dinnensional inner product space is called positive definite [positive semidefinite] if T is self-adjoint. and \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), x\rangle>0[\langle\mathrm{~T}(x), x\rangle \geq 0]\) for all \(x \neq 0\).

    An \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) with entrics from \(R\) or \(C\) is called positive definite [positive semidefinite] if \(\mathrm{L}_{A}\) is positive definite [positive semidcfirite]. 17. Let \(T\) and \(U\) be a self-adjoint linear operators on an \(n\)-dinensional inner product space \(V\), and let \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\), where \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis for \(V\). Prove the following results. (a) T is positive definite [semidefinite] if and only if all of its cigenvalnes are positive [nonnegalive]. (b) T is positive definite if and only if

    \[ \sum_{i, j} \Lambda_{i j} a_{j} \bar{a}_{i}>0 \text { for all nonzero } n \text {-tuples }\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n}\right) . \]

    (c) T is positive semidefinite if and only if \(A=B^{*} B\) for some square matrix \(B\). (d) If T and U are positive semidefinite operators such that \(\mathrm{T}^{2}=\mathrm{U}^{2}\), then \(T=U\). (e) If \(T\) and \(U\) are positive definite operators such that \(T U=U T\), then TU is positive definite. (f) T is positive definite [semidefinite] if and only if \(A\) is positive definite [semidefinite]. Becanse of (f). results analogous to itcms (a) throngh (d) hold for matrices as well as operators. 18. Let \(T: V \rightarrow W\) be a linear transformation, where \(V\) and \(W\) are finitedimensional inner product spaces. Prove the following results. (a) TT and TT are positive somidefinite. (See Exercise 15 of Section 6.3.) (b) \(\operatorname{rank}\left(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\right)=\operatorname{rank}\left(\mathrm{TT}^{*}\right)=\operatorname{rank}(\mathrm{T})\). 19. Let \(T\) and \(U\) be positive definite operators on an inner product space \(V\). Prove the following results. (a) \(\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{U}\) is positive definite. (b) If \(c>0\), then \(c T\) is positive definite. (c) \(\mathrm{T}^{-1}\) is positive definite. 20. Let \(V\) be an inner product space with inner product \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\), and let \(T\) be a positive definite linear operator on V . Prove that \(\langle x, y\rangle^{\prime}=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle\) defines another inmer product on V . 21. Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space, and let \(T\) and \(U\) be self-adjoint operators on \(V\) such that \(T\) is positive definite. Prove that both TU and UT are diagonalizable linear operators that have only real eigenvalues. Hint: Show that UT is self-adjoint with respect to the inner product \(\langle x, y\rangle^{\prime}=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle\). To show that TU is solf-adjoint, repeat the arghment with \(\mathrm{T}^{-1}\) in place of T . 22. This exercise provides a converse to Exercise 20. Let V be a finitedimensional inner product space with inner product \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\), and let \(\langle\cdot: \cdot\rangle^{\prime}\) be any other inner product on \(V\). (a) Prove that there exists a unique linear operator \(T\) on \(V\) such that \(\langle x, y\rangle^{\prime}=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle\) for all \(x\) and \(y\) in V . Hint: Let \(\beta=\) \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for V with respect to \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\), and define a matrix \(A\) by \(A_{i j}=\left\langle v_{j}, v_{i}\right\rangle^{\prime}\) for all \(i\) and \(j\). Lët T be the unique linear operator on V such that \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}=A\). (b) Prove that the operator \(T\) of (a) is positive definite with respect to both inner products. 23. Let \(U\) be a diagonalizable linear opcrator on a finite-dimensional inner product space \(V\) such that all of the eigenvalues of \(U\) are real. Prove that there exist positive definite linear operators \(T_{1}\) and \(T_{1}^{\prime}\) and self-adjoint linear operators \(\mathrm{T}_{2}\) and \(\mathrm{T}_{2}^{\prime}\) such that \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{1}=\mathrm{T}_{1}^{\prime} \mathrm{T}_{2}^{\prime}\). Hint: Let \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) be the inner product associated with \(\mathrm{V}, \beta\) a basis of eigenvectors for U , \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle^{\prime}\) the inner product on \(V\) with respect to which \(\beta\) is orthonormal (see Exercise 22(a) of Section 6.1), and \(T_{1}\) the positive definite operator according to Exercise 22. Show that U is self-adjoint with respect to \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle^{\prime}\) and \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T}_{1}^{-1} \mathrm{U}^{*} \mathrm{~T}_{1}\) (the adjoint is with respect to \(\langle\cdot, \cdot\rangle\) ). Let \(\mathrm{T}_{2}=\mathrm{T}_{1}{ }^{-1} \mathrm{U}^{*}\). 24. This argment gives another proof of Schur's theorem. Let T be a linear operator on a finite dimensional inner product space \(V\). (a) Suppose that \(\beta\) is an ordered basis for \(V\) such that \([T]_{\beta}\) is an upper triangular matrix. Let \(\gamma\) be the orthonormal basis for \(V\) obtained by applying the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process to \(\beta\) and then normalizing the resulting vectors. Prove that \([\mathrm{T}]_{\gamma}\) is an upper triangular matrix. (b) Use Exercise 32 of Section 5.4 and (a) to obtain an alternate proof of Schur's theorem.

    6.5 UNITARY AND ORTHOGONAL OPERATORS AND THEIR MATRICES¶

    In this section, we continue our analogy between complex numbers and linear operators. Recall that the adjoint of a linear operator acts similarly to the conjugate of a complex number (see, for example, Theorem 6.11 p. 359). A complex number \(z\) has length 1 if \(z \bar{z}=1\). In this section, we study those linear operators T on an imer product space V such that \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{I}\). We will see that thesc are precisely the linear operators that "preserve length" in the sense that \(\|T(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). As another characterization, we prove that, on a finite-dimensional complex inmer product space, these are the normal operators whose eigenvalues all have absolute value 1.

    In past chapters, we were interested in studying those functions that preserve the structure of the underlying space. In particular, linear operators preserve the operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication, and isomorphisins preserve all the vector space structure. It is now natural to consider those lincar operators \(T\) on an inner product space that preserve length. We will see that this condition guarantees, in fact, that \(T\) preserves the inner product.

    Definitions. Let T be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V (over \(F\) ). If \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we call T a unitary operator if \(F=C\) and an orthogonal operator if \(F=R\).

    It shoukd be noted that, in the infinite-dinensional case, an operator satisfying the preceding norm requirenent is generally called an isometry. If, in addition. the operator is onto (the condition guarantees onc-to-one), then the operator is called a unitary or orthogonal operator.

    Clearly, any rotation or reflection in \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) presorves length and hence is an orthogonal operator. We study these operators in much more detail in Section 6.11.

    Example 1¶

    Let \(h \in \mathbf{H}\) satisfy \(|h(x)|=1\) for all \(x\). Define the linear operator T on H by \(\mathrm{T}(f)=h f\). Then

    \[ \|\mathrm{T}(f)\|^{2}=\|h f\|^{2}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} h(t) f(t) \overline{h(t) f(t)} d t=\|f\|^{2} \]

    since \(|h(t)|^{2}=1\) for all \(t\). So T is a unitary operator. Theorem 6.18. Let T be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V . Then the following statements are equivalent. (a) \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{I}\). (b) \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). (c) If \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis for V , then \(\mathrm{T}(\beta)\) is an orthonormal basis for V . (d) There exists an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for V such that \(\mathrm{T}(\beta)\) is an orthonormal basis for V . (e) \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\).

    Thus all the conditions above are equivalent to the definition of a unitary or orthogonal operator. From (a), it follows that unitary or orthogonal operators are normal.

    Before proving the theorem, we first prove a lemma. Compare this lemma to Exercise 11(b) of Section 6.4.

    Lemma. Let U be a self-adjoint operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V . If \(\langle x, \mathrm{U}(x)\rangle=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), then \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\).

    Proof. By either Theorem 6.16 (p. 372) or 6.17 (p. 374), we may choose an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for V consisting of eigenvectors of U . If \(x \in \beta\), then \(\mathrm{U}(x)=\lambda x\) for some \(\lambda\). Thus

    \[ 0=\langle x, \cup(x)\rangle=\langle x, \lambda x\rangle=\bar{\lambda}\langle x, x\rangle \]

    so \(\bar{\lambda}=0\). Hence \(\mathrm{U}(x)=0\) for all \(x \in \beta\), and thus, \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\). Proof of Theorem 6.18. We prove first that (a) implies (b). Let \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Then \(\langle x, y\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}(x), y\right\rangle=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle\).

    Second, we prove that (b) implies (c). Let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for V ; so \(\mathrm{T}(\beta)=\left\{\mathrm{T}\left(v_{1}\right), \mathrm{T}\left(v_{2}\right), \ldots, \mathrm{T}\left(v_{n}\right)\right\}\). It follows that \(\left\langle\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right), \mathrm{T}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle=\delta_{i j}\). Thercfore \(\mathrm{T}(\beta)\) is an orthonormal basis for V .

    That (c) implics (d) is obvious. Next we prove that (d) implies (e). Let \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), and let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\). Now

    \[ x=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} v_{i} \]

    for some scalars \(a_{i}\), and so

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|x\|^{2} & =\left\langle\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} v_{i}, \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{j} v_{j}\right\rangle=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{i} \overline{a_{j}}\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle \\ & =\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{i} \overline{a_{j}} \delta_{i j}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}\right|^{2} \end{aligned} \]

    since \(\beta\) is orthonormal. Applying the same manipulations to

    \[ \mathrm{T}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right) \]

    and using the fact that \(T(\beta)\) is also orthonormal, we obtain

    \[ \|\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|a_{i}\right|^{2} . \]

    Hence \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\). Finally, we prove that (e) implies (a). For any \(x \in V\), we have

    \[ \langle x, x\rangle=\|x\|^{2}=\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2}=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(x)\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}(x)\right\rangle \]

    So \(\left\langle x,\left(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\right)(x)\right\rangle=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Let \(\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\); then \(ل\) _is selfadjoint, and \(\langle x, \mathrm{U}(x)\rangle=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Hence, by the lemma, we have \(T_{0}=U=I-T^{*} T\), and therefore \(T^{*} T=I\). Since \(V\) is finite-dimensional, we may use Exercise 10 of Section 2.4 to conclude that TT* \(=\mathrm{I}\).

    It follows immediately from the definition that every eigenvalue of a unitary or orthogonal operator has absolute value 1 . In fact, even more is true.

    Corollary 1. Let T be a linear opcrator on a finite-dimensional real inner product space V . Then \(\vee\) has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of T with corresponding eigenvalues of absolute value 1 if and only if T is both self-adjoint and orthogonal.

    Proof. Suppose that V has an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) such that \(T\left(v_{i}\right)=\lambda_{i} v_{i}\) and \(\left|\lambda_{i}\right|=1\) for all \(i\). By Theorem 6.17 (p. 374), \(T\) is self-adjoint. Thus \(\left(\mathrm{TT}^{*}\right)\left(v_{i}\right)=\mathrm{T}\left(\lambda_{i} v_{i}\right)=\lambda_{i} \lambda_{i} v_{i}=\lambda_{i}^{2} v_{i}=v_{i}\) for each \(i\). So \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}=\mathrm{I}\), and again by Exercise 10 of Section 2.4, T is orthogonal by Theorem 6.18(a).

    If T is self-adjoint, then, by Theorem 6.17 , we have that V possesses an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) such that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\lambda_{i} v_{i}\) for all \(i\). If T is also orthogonal, we have

    \[ \left|\lambda_{i}\right| \cdot\left\|v_{i}\right\|=\left\|\lambda_{i} v_{i}\right\|=\left\|\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)\right\|=\left\|v_{i}\right\| ; \]

    so \(\left|\lambda_{i}\right|=1\) for every \(i\).

    Corollary 2. Let T be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space \(V\). Then \(\vee\) has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of \(T\) with corresponding eigenvalues of absolute value 1 if and only if T is unitary.

    Proof. The proof is similar to the proof of Corollary 1.

    Example 2¶

    Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{2}\) be a rotation by \(\theta\), where \(0<\theta<\pi\). It is clear geometrically that T "preserves length," that is, that \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{R}^{2}\). The fact that rotations by a fixed angle preserve perpendicularity not only can be seen geometrically but now follows from (b) of Theorem 6.18. Perhaps the fact that such a transformation preserves the inner product is not so obvious; however, we obtain this fact from (b) also. Finally, an inspection of the matrix representation of \(T\) with respect to the standard ordered basis, which is

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right) \]

    reveals that \(T\) is not self-adjoint for the given restriction on \(\theta\). As we mentioned earlier, this fact also follows from the geometric observation that T has no eigenvectors and from Theorem 6.15 (p. 371). It is seen easily from the preceding matrix that \(T^{*}\) is the rotation by \(-\theta\).

    Definition. Let \(L\) be a one-dimensional subspace of \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). We may view L as a line in the plane through the origin. A linear operator T on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) is called a reflection of \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) about L if \(\mathrm{T}(x)=x\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~L}\) and \(\mathrm{T}(x)=-x\) for all \(x \in L^{\perp}\).

    As an example of a reflection, consider the operator defined in Example 3 of Section 2.5.

    Example 3¶

    Let \(T\) be a reflection of \(R^{2}\) about a line \(L\) through the origin. We show that T is an orthogonal operator. Select vectors \(v_{1} \in \mathrm{~L}\) and \(v_{2} \in \mathrm{~L}^{\perp}\) such that \(\left\|v_{1}\right\|=\left\|v_{2}\right\|=1\). Then \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{1}\right)=v_{1}\) and \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{2}\right)=-v_{2}\). Thus \(v_{1}\) and \(v_{2}\) are eigenvectors of \(T\) with corresponding eigenvalues 1 and -1 , respectively. Furthermore, \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). It follows that T is an orthogonal operator by Corollary 1 to Theorem 6.18.

    We now examine the matrices that represent unitary and orthogonal transformations.

    Definitions. A square matrix \(A\) is called an orthogonal matrix if \(A^{t} A=A A^{t}=I\) and unitary if \(A^{*} A=A A^{*}=I\).

    Since for a real matrix \(A\) we have \(A^{*}=A^{t}\), a real unitary matrix is also orthogonal. In this case, we call \(A\) orthogonal rather than unitary.

    Note that the condition \(A A^{*}=I\) is equivalent to the statement that the rows of \(A\) form an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\) because

    \[ \delta_{i j}=I_{i j}=\left(A A^{*}\right)_{i j}=\sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{i k}\left(A^{*}\right)_{k j}=\sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{i k} \overline{A_{j k}} \]

    and the last term represents the inner product of the \(i\) th and \(j\) th rows of \(A\). A similar remark can be made about the columns of \(A\) and the condition \(A^{*} A=I\).

    It also follows from the definition above and from Theorem 6.10 (p. 359) that a lincar operator \(T\) on an inner product space V is unitary [orthogonal] if and only if \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\) is unitary [orthogonal] for some orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for V .

    Example 4¶

    From Example 2, the matrix

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right) \]

    is clearly orthogonal. One can easily see that the rows of the matrix form an orthonormal basis for \(R^{2}\). Similarly, the columns of the matrix form an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\).

    Example 5¶

    Let \(T\) be a reflection of \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) about a line L through the origin, let \(\beta\) be the standard ordered basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\), and let \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\). Then \(T=L_{A}\). Since \(T\) is an orthogonal operator and \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis, \(A\) is an orthogonal matrix. We describe \(A\).

    Suppose that \(\alpha\) is the angle from the positive \(x\)-axis to L. Let \(v_{1}=\) \((\cos \alpha, \sin \alpha)\) and \(v_{2}=(-\sin \alpha, \cos \alpha)\). Then \(\left\|v_{1}\right\|=\left\|v_{2}\right\|=1, v_{1} \in \mathrm{~L}\), and \(v_{2} \in \mathrm{~L}^{\perp}\). Hence \(\gamma=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). Because \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{1}\right)=v_{1}\) and \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{2}\right)=-v_{2}\), we have

    \[ [\mathrm{T}]_{\gamma}=\left[\mathrm{L}_{A}\right]_{\gamma}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right) \]

    Let

    \[ Q=\left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right) . \]

    By the corollary to Theorem 2.23 (p. 115),

    \[ A=Q\left[\mathrm{~L}_{A}\right]_{\gamma} Q^{-1} \]
    \[ \begin{aligned} & =\left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{rr} \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right) \\ & =\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos ^{2} \alpha-\sin ^{2} \alpha & 2 \sin \alpha \cos \alpha \\ 2 \sin \cos \alpha & -\left(\cos ^{2} \alpha-\sin ^{2} \alpha\right) \end{array}\right) \\ & =\left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos 2 \alpha & \sin 2 \alpha \\ \sin 2 \alpha & -\cos 2 \alpha \end{array}\right) . \end{aligned} \]

    We know that. for a complex normal [real symmetric] matrix \(A\). there exists an orthomomal hasis if for \(F^{\prime \prime}\) consisting of cigenvectors of \(A\). Hence \(A\) is similar to a diagonal matrix \(D\). B; the corollary to Theorem 2.23 (p. 115). the matrix \(Q\) whese columns are the vectors in 3 is such that \(D=Q^{-1} A Q\). But since the columns of \(Q\) are an orthomormal basis for \(F^{n}\), it follows that \(Q\) is unitary [orthogonal]. In this case, we say that \(A\) is unitarily equivalent [orthogonally equivalent] to \(D\). It is oasily seen (see Exercise 18) that this relation is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}\left(C^{\prime}\right)\left[\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(R)\right]\). More generally. \(A\) and \(B\) are unitarily cquicalent [orthougonally cquinalent] if and only if there exists a unitary [orthogonal] matrix \(P\) such that \(A=P^{*} B P\).

    The preceding paragraph has proved half of each of the next two theorems.

    Théorem 6.19. Let \(A\) be a complex \(n \times n\) matrix. Then \(A\) is normal if and only if \(A\) is mitarily equivalent to a diagonal matrix.

    Proof. By the preceding remarks, we need only prove that if \(A\) is unitarily equivalent to a diagonal matrix. then \(A\) is nomal.

    Suppose that \(A=P^{*} D P\), where \(P\) is a unitary matrix and \(D\) is a diagonal matrix. Then

    \[ A A^{*}=\left(P^{*} D P\right)\left(P^{*} D P\right)^{*}=\left(P^{*} D \Gamma^{P}\right)\left(I^{*} D^{*} P\right)=I^{*} D I D^{*} I=I^{*} D D^{*} P \]

    Similarly, \(A^{*} A=P^{*} D^{*} D P\). Since \(D\) is a diagonal matrix, however, we have \(D D^{*}=D^{*} D\). Thus \(A A^{*}=A^{*} A\).

    Theorem 6.20. Let \(A\) be a real \(n \times n\) matris. Then \(A\) is symmetric if and only if \(A\) is orthogonally equivalent to a real diagomal matrix.

    Proof. The proof is similar to the proof of Theorem 6.19 and is left as an exercise.

    Example 6¶

    Let

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right) \]

    Since \(A\) is symmetric, Theorem 6.20 tells us that \(A\) is orthogonally equivalent to a diagonal matrix. We find an orthogonal matrix \(P\) and a diagonal matrix \(D\) such that \(P^{t} A P=D\).

    To find \(P\), we obtain an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors. It is easy to show that the cigenvalues of \(A\) are 2 and 8 . The set \(\{(-1,1,0),(-1,0,1)\}\) is a basis for the eigenspace corresponding to 2 . Because this set is not orthogonal, we apply the Gram Schnidt process to obtain the orthogonal set \(\left\{(-1,1,0),-\frac{1}{2}(1,1,-2)\right\}\). The set \(\{(1,1,1)\}\) is a basis for the cigenspace corresponding to 8 . Notice that (1.1.1) is orthogonal to the preceding two vectors, as predicted by Theorem \(6.15(\mathrm{~d})\) (p. 371). Taking the mion of these two bases and normalizing the vectors, we obtain the following orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\) consisting of eigenvectors of \(A\) :

    \[ \left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1,1,0) ; \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1,1,-2) ; \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)\right\} . \]

    Thus one possible choice for \(P\) is

    \[ P=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ 0 & \frac{-2}{\sqrt{6}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{array}\right), \quad \text { and } \quad D=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 8 \end{array}\right) . \]

    Because of Schur's theorem (Theorem 6.14 p. 370): the nexf Tesult is immediate. As it is the matrix form of Schmes theorem, we also refer to it as Schur's theorem.

    Theorem 6.21 (Schur). Let \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) be a matrix whose characteristic polynomial splits ower \(F\). (a) If \(F=C\). then \(A\) is unitarily cquivalent to a complex upper triangular matrix. (b) If \(F=R\), then \(A\) is orthogonally equivalent to a roal upper triangular matrix.

    Rigid Motions*¶

    The purpose of this application is to characterize the so-called rigid motions of a finite-dimensional real inner product space. Onc may think intuitively of such a motion as a transformation that does not affect the shape of a figure under its action, hence the term rigid. The key reguirement for such a transfomation is that it preserves distauces.

    Definition. Let \(\vee\) be a real inner product space. A function \(f: V \rightarrow \mathrm{~V}\) is called a rigid motion if

    \[ \|f(x)-f(y)\|=\|x-y\| \]

    for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). For example, any orthogonal operator on a finite-dimensional real inner product space is a rigid motion.

    Another class of rigid motions are the translations. A function \(g: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\), where \(V\) is a real inner product space, is called a translation if there exists a vector \(v_{0} \in \mathrm{~V}\) such that \(g(x)=x+v_{0}\) for all. \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). We say that \(g\) is the translation by \(v_{0}\). It is a simple exercise to show that translations, as well as composites of rigid motions on a real inner product space. are also rigid motions. (See Exercise 22.) Thus an orthogonal operator on a finitedimensional real inner product space \(V\) followed by a translation on \(V\) is a rigid motion on \(V\). Remarkably, every rigid motion on \(V\) may be characterized in this way.

    Theorem 6.22. Let \(f: \vee \rightarrow \vee\) be a rigid motion on a finite-dimensional real inner product space V . Then there exists a unique orthogonal operator \(T\) on \(V\) and a unique translation \(g\) on \(V\) such that \(f=g \circ T\).

    Any orthogonal operator is a special case of this composite, in which the translation is by 0 . Any translation is also a special case, in which the orthogonal operator is the identity operator.

    Proof. Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) be defined by \(\mathrm{T}(x)=f(x)-f(0)\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). We show that T is an orthogonal operator, from which it follows that \(f=g \circ \mathrm{~T}\), where \(g\) is the translation by \(f(0)\). Observe that T is the composite of \(f\) and the translation by \(-f(0)\); hence T is a rigid motion. Furthermore, for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\)

    \[ \|T(x)\|^{2}=\|f(x)-f(0)\|^{2}=\|x-0\|^{2}=\|x\|^{2} \]

    and consequently \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Thus for any \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\),

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|\mathrm{T}(x)-\mathrm{T}(y)\|^{2} & =\|\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2}-2\langle\mathrm{~T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle+\|\mathrm{T}(y)\|^{2} \\ & =\|x\|^{2}-2\langle\mathrm{~T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle+\|y\|^{2} \end{aligned} \]

    and

    \[ \|x-y\|^{2}=\|x\|^{2}-2\langle x, y\rangle+\|y\|^{2} . \]

    But \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)-\mathrm{T}(y)\|^{2}=\|x-y\|^{2}\); so \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). We are now in a position to show that T is a linear transformation. Let \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), and let \(a \in R\). Then

    \[ \|\mathrm{T}(x+a y)-\mathrm{T}(x)-a \mathrm{~T}(y)\|^{2}=\|[\mathrm{T}(x+a y)-\mathrm{T}(x)]-a \mathrm{~T}(y)\|^{2} \]
    \[ \begin{aligned} & =\|\mathrm{T}(x+a y)-\mathrm{T}(x)\|^{2}+a^{2}\|\mathrm{~T}(y)\|^{2}-2 a\langle\mathrm{~T}(x+a y)-\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle \\ & =\|(x+a y)-x\|^{2}+a^{2}\|y\|^{2}-2 a[\langle\mathrm{~T}(x+a y), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle-\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle] \\ & =a^{2}\|y\|^{2}+a^{2}\|y\|^{2}-2 a[\langle x+a y, y\rangle-\langle x, y\rangle] \\ & =2 a^{2}\|y\|^{2}-2 a\left[\langle x, y\rangle+a\|y\|^{2}-\langle x, y\rangle\right] \\ & =0 . \end{aligned} \]

    Thus \(\mathrm{T}(x+a y)=\mathrm{T}(x)+a \mathrm{~T}(y)\), and hence T is lincar. Since T also preserves inner products, T is an orthogonal operator.

    To prove uniqueness, suppose that \(u_{0}\) and \(v_{0}\) are in V and T and U are orthogonal operators on V such that,

    \[ f(x)=\mathrm{T}(x)+u_{0}=\mathrm{U}(x)+v_{0} \]

    for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Substituting \(x=0\) in the preceding equation yields \(u_{0}=v_{0}\), and hence the translation is unique. This cquation, therefore, reduces to \(\mathrm{T}(x)=\mathrm{U}(x)\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), and hence \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{U}\).

    Orthogonal Operators on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\)¶

    Because of Theorem 6.22, an understanding of rigid motions requires a characterization of orthogonal operators. The next result characterizes orthogonal operators on \(R^{2}\). We postpone the case of orthogonal operators on more general spaces to Section 6.11.

    Theorem 6.23. Let T be an orthogonal opcrator on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\), and let \(A=[\mathrm{T}]_{\mathcal{B}}\), where \(\beta\) is the standard ordered basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). Then exactly one of the following conditions is satisfied: (a) T is a rotation, and \(\operatorname{det}(\mathrm{A})=1\). (b) T is a reflection about a line through the origin, and \(\operatorname{det}(\mathrm{A})=-1\).

    Proof. Because T is an orthogonal operator, \(\mathrm{T}(\beta)=\left\{\mathrm{T}\left(e_{1}\right), \mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) by Theorem \(6.18(\mathrm{c})\). Since \(T\left(e_{1}\right)\) is a unit vector, there is a unique angle \(\theta, 0 \leq \theta<2 \pi\), such that \(\mathrm{T}\left(e_{1}\right)=(\cos \theta, \sin \theta)\). Since \(\mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)\) is a unit vector and is orthogonal to \(\mathrm{T}\left(e_{1}\right)\), there are only two possible choices for \(\mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)\). Either

    \[ \mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)=(-\sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta) \quad \text { or } \quad \mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)=(\sin \theta,-\cos \theta) \]

    First. suppose that \(\mathrm{T}\left(e_{2}\right)=(-\sin \theta, \cos \theta)\). Then \(A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}\cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta\end{array}\right)\). It follows from Example 1 of Section 6.4 that \(T\) is a rotation by the angle \(\theta\). Also

    \[ \operatorname{det}(A)=\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1 \]

    Now suppose that \(\mathrm{T}\left(\dot{e}_{2}\right)=(\sin \dot{\theta},-\cos \theta)\). Then \(A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}\cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & -\cos \theta\end{array}\right)\). Comparing this matrix to the matrix \(A\) of Example 5, we see that T is the reflection of \(R^{2}\) about a line \(L\), so that \(\alpha=\theta / 2\) is the angle from the positive \(x\)-axis to L. Furthermore,

    \[ \operatorname{det}(A)=-\cos ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta=-1 \]

    Combining Theorems 6.22 and 6.23 , we obtain the following characterization of rigid motions on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\).

    Corollary. Any rigid motion on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) is either a rotation followed by a translation or a reflection about a line through the origin followed by a translation.

    Example 7¶

    Let

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \\ \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}} \end{array}\right) \]

    We show, that \(L_{A}\) is the reflection of \(R^{2}\) about a line \(L\) through the origin, and then describe L .

    Clearly \(A A^{*}=A^{*} A=I\), and therefore \(A\) is an orthogonal matrix. Hence \(\mathrm{L}_{A}\) is an orthogonal operator. Furthermore,

    \[ \operatorname{det}(A)=-\frac{1}{5}-\frac{4}{5}=-1 \]

    and thus \(L_{A}\) is a reflection of \(R^{2}\) about a line \(L\) through the origin by Theorem 6.23. Since \(L\) is the one-dimensional eigenspace corresponding to the eigenvalue 1 of \(L_{A}\), it suffices to find an eigenvector of \(L_{A}\) corresponding to 1 . One such vector is \(v=(2, \sqrt{5}-1)\). Thus L is the span of \(\{v\}\). Alternatively, \(L\) is the line through the origin with slope \((\sqrt{5}-1) / 2\), and hence is the line with the equation

    \[ y=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} x \]

    Conic Sections¶

    As an application of Theorem 6.20, we consider the quadratic equation

    \[ \begin{equation*} a x^{2}+2 b x y+c y^{2}+d x+e y+f=0 . \tag{2} \end{equation*} \]

    For special choices of the coefficients in (2), we obtain the various conic sections. For example, if \(a=c=1, b=d=e=0\), and \(f=-1\), we obtain the circle \(x^{2}+y^{2}=1\) with center at the origin. The remaining conic sections, namely, the cllipse, parabola, and hyperbola, are obtained by other choices of the coefficients. If \(b=0\), then it is easy to graph the equation by the method of completing the square because the \(x y\)-term is absent. For example, the equation \(x^{2}+2 x+y^{2}+4 y+2=0\) may be rewritten as \((x+1)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}=3\), which describes a circle with radius \(\sqrt{3}\) and center at \((-1,-2)\) in the \(x y\)-coordinate system. If we consider the transformation of coordinates \((x, y) \rightarrow\left(x^{\prime}, y^{\prime}\right)\), where \(x^{\prime}=x+1\) and \(y^{\prime}=y+2\), then our equation simplifies to \(\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}=3\). This change of variable allows us to eliminate the \(x\) - and \(y\)-terms.

    We now concentrate solely on the elimination of the \(x y\)-term. To accomplish this, we consider the expression

    \[ \begin{equation*} a x^{2}+2 b x y+c y^{2} \tag{3} \end{equation*} \]

    which is called the associated quadratic form of (2). Quadratic forms are studied in more generality in Section 6.8.

    If we let

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ b & c \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad X=\binom{x}{y} \]

    then (3) may be written as \(X^{t} A X=\langle A X, X\rangle\). For example, the quadratic form \(3 x^{2}+4 x y+6 y^{2}\) may be written as

    \[ X^{t}\left(\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 6 \end{array}\right) X \]

    The fact that \(A\) is symmetric is crucial in our discussion. For, by Theorem 6.20, we may choose an orthogonal matrix \(P\) and a diagonal matrix \(D\) with real diagonal entries \(\lambda_{1}\) and \(\lambda_{2}\) such that \(P^{t} A P=D\). Now define

    \[ X^{\prime}=\binom{x^{\prime}}{y^{\prime}} \]

    by \(X^{\prime}=P^{t} X\) or, equivalently, by \(P X^{\prime}=P P^{t} X=X\). Then

    \[ X^{t} A X=\left\langle P X^{\prime}\right)^{t} A\left(P X^{\prime}\right)=X^{\prime t}\left(P^{t} A P\right) X^{\prime}=X^{\prime t} D X^{\prime}=\lambda_{1}\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2} \]

    Thus the transformation \((x, y) \rightarrow\left(x^{\prime}, y^{\prime}\right)\) allows us to eliminate the \(x y\)-term in (3), and hence in (2).

    Furthermore, since \(P\) is orthogonal, we have by Theorem 6.23 (with \(\mathrm{T}=\) \(\mathrm{L}_{P}\) ) that \(\operatorname{det}(P)= \pm 1\). If \(\operatorname{det}(P)=-1\), we may interchange the columns of \(P\) to obtain a matrix \(Q\). Because the columns of \(P\) form an orthonomal basis of eigenvectors of \(A\). the same is true of the columns of \(Q\). Therefore,

    \[ Q^{t} A Q=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \lambda_{2} & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_{1} \end{array}\right) . \]

    Notice that \(\operatorname{det}(Q)=-\operatorname{det}(P)=1\). So, if det \((P)=-1\), we can take \(Q\) for our new \(I\); consecpuently, we may always choose \(P\) so that \(\operatorname{det}(P)=1\). By Lemma 4 to Theorem 6.22 (with \(T=L_{P}\) ), it follows that matrix \(P\) represents a rotation.

    In summary. the ry-term in (2) may be climinated by a rotation of the \(x\)-axis and \(y\)-axis to now axes \(x^{\prime}\) and \(y^{\prime}\) given by \(X=P X^{\prime}\). where \(P\) is an orthogonal matrix and \(\operatorname{det}(P)=1\). Furthermore. the cocfficients of \(\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}\) and \(\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}\) are the cigenvalues of

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ b & c \end{array}\right) . \]

    This result is a restatement of a result known as the principal axis theorem for \(R^{2}\). The arguments above, of course, are casily extended to quadratic equations in \(n\) variables. For example, in the case \(n=3\), by special choices of the coefficients, we obtain the quadratic surfaces the elliptic cone. the ellipsoid. the hyperbolic paraboloid. etc.

    As an illustration of the preceding transformation. consider the quadratic equation

    \[ 2 x^{2}-4 x y+5 y^{2}-36=0 \]

    for which the associated pualratic form is \(2 x^{2}-4 x y+5 y y^{2}\). In the notation we have been using,

    \[ A=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 5 \end{array}\right) \]

    so that the eigenvalues of \(A\) are 1 and 6 with associated eigenvectors

    \[ \binom{2}{1} \text { and }\binom{-1}{2} . \]

    As expected (from Theorem 6.15(d) p. 371), these vectors are orthogonal. The corresponding orthonormal basis of eigenvectors

    \[ 3=\left\{\binom{\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}} \cdot\binom{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}}\right\} \]

    determines new axes. \(x^{\prime}\) and \(y^{\prime}\) as in Figure ( \(\mathbf{j}\). 1 . Hence if

    \[ P=\left(\begin{array}{ll} \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right) \]

    then

    \[ P^{\prime} A I^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{array}\right) \]

    Cuder the transformation \(X=P \cdot X^{\prime}\) or

    \[ \begin{aligned} & x=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, x^{\prime}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} y^{\prime} \\ & y=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot x^{\prime}+\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}!^{\prime} \end{aligned} \]

    we hate the new quadratice form \(\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+6\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}\). Thus the original equation \(2 r^{2}-4 x y+5 y^{2}=36\) may be writien in the form \(\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+6\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}=36\) relative to a new coordinates system with the \(\boldsymbol{r}^{\prime}\) - and ! \(\prime^{\prime}\)-axes in the directions of the first and secome vertors of ; , respectively. It is clear that this equation represents

    Figure 6.4 an ellipse. (Sce Figure 6.4.) Note that the preveding matrix I' has the form

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{cc} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{array}\right) \]

    where \(\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 26.6^{\circ}\). So \(P\) is the matrix represemation of at rotation of \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) through the angle \(\theta\). Thus the change of variable \(X=I^{\prime} X^{\prime}\) can be accomplished by this rotation of the \(r\) - and \(y\)-axes. There is another possilitity for \(l\). however. If the eigenvector of \(A\) corresponding to the cigenvalue 6 is laken to be \((1,-2)\) instead of \((-1,2)\), and the sigenvalues are interchanged. then we obtain the matrix

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \\ \frac{-2}{\sqrt{5}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \end{array}\right) \]

    which is the matrix representation of a rotation through the angle \(\theta=\) \(\sin ^{-1}\left(-\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right) \approx-63.4^{c}\). This posisibility produces the same ellipse as the one in Figure 6.4, but interchanges the names of the \(x^{\prime}\) - and \(y^{\prime}\)-axes.

    EXERCISES¶

    1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying inner product spaces are finite-dimensional. (a) Every unilary operator is nomal. (b) Every orthogomal operator is diagonalizable. (c) A matrix is unitary if and only if it is insertible. (d) If two matrices are mitarily equivalent, hen they are also similar. (e) The sum of mitary matriers is mitary. (f) The adjoint of a mitary operator is unitary. (g) If T is an orthogonal operator on V . then \([\mathrm{T}]_{.1}\) is an orthogonal matrix for auy ordered basis. 3 for V . (h) If all the eigenvalues of a linear operator are 1 . then the operator must be unitary or orthogonal. (i) A lincar operator may preserve the norm. but not the inmer prodnet.
    2. For cach of the following matrices A. find an orthogonal or mitary matrix \(P\) and a diagonal matrix \(I\) such that \(P^{\wedge} A P^{\prime}=D\). (a) \(\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right)\) (b) \(\left(\begin{array}{rr}0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right)\) (c) \(\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 3-3 i \\ 3+3 i & 5\end{array}\right)\) (d) \(\left(\begin{array}{lll}0 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 0\end{array}\right)\) (c) \(\left(\begin{array}{lll}2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right)\)
    3. Prove that the composite of unitary [orthogomal] operators is unitary [orthogonal].
    4. For \(z \in C\), define \(\mathrm{T}_{z}: C \rightarrow C\) by \(\mathrm{T}_{z}(u)=z u\). Charactcrize those \(z\) for which \(T_{z}\) is normal, self-adjoint, or unitary.
    5. Which of the following pairs of matrices are unitarily equivalent? (a) \(\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)\) and \(\left(\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right)\) \(\left.\begin{array}{l}\text { (b) } \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0\end{array}\right)\) (c) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right)\) (d) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right)\) (e) \(\left(\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right) \quad\) and \(\quad\left(\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3\end{array}\right)\)
    6. Let \(V\) be the inner product space of complex-valued continuous functions on \([0,1]\) with the imer product
    \[ \langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) \overline{g(t)} d t \]

    Let \(h \in V\), and define \(T: V \rightarrow V\) by \(T(f)=h f\). Prove that \(T\) is a unitary operator if and only if \(|h(t)|=1\) for \(0 \leq t \leq 1\). 7. Prove that if T is a unitary operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V , then T has a unitary square root: that is, there exists a unitary operator U such that \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{U}^{2}\). 8. Let \(T\) be a self-adjoint linear operator on a finite-dimensional immer product space. Prove that \((T+i l)(T-i l)^{-1}\) is mitary using Exercise 10 of Section 6.4. 9. Let U be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional imner product space V . If \(\|\mathrm{U}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x\) in some orthonormal basis for V , must U be unitary? Justify your answer with a proof or a counterexample. 10. Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) real symmetric or complex normal matrix. Prove that

    \[ \operatorname{tr}(A)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_{i} \quad \text { and } \quad \operatorname{tr}\left(A^{*} A\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\lambda_{i}\right|^{2} ; \]

    where the \(\lambda_{i}\) 's are the (not necessarily distinct) eigenvalues of \(A\). 11. Find an orthogonal matrix whose first row is ( \(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{3}\) ). 12. Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) real symmetric or complex normal matrix. Prove that

    \[ \operatorname{det}(A)=\prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_{i} \]

    where the \(\lambda_{\text {, }}\) 's are the (not necessarily distinct) eigenvalues of \(A\). 13. Suppose that \(A\) and \(B\) are diagonalizable matrices. Prove or disprove that \(A\) is similar to \(B\) if and only if \(A\) and \(B\) are unitarily equivalent. 14. Prove that if \(A\) and \(B\) are unitarily equivalent matrices, then \(A\) is positive definite [semidefinite] if and only if \(B\) is positive definite [semidefinite]. (See the definitions in the exercises in Section 6.4.) 15. Let \(U\) be a mitary operator on an inner product space \(V\). and let \(W\) be a finite-dimensional \(U\)-invariant subspace of \(V\). Prove that (a) \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{W})=\mathrm{W}\) : (b) \(\mathrm{W}^{\mathrm{L}}\) is U -invariant.

    Contrast (b) with Exercise 16.

    \(-4\)¶

    1. Find an example of a unitary operator \(U\) on an inner product space and a U -invariant subspace W such that \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\) is not U -invariant.
    2. Prove that a matrix that is both unitary and upper triangular must be a diagonal matrix.
    3. Show that "is unitarily equivalent to" is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(C)\).
    4. Let \(W\) be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product sipace \(V\). By Theorem 6.7 (p. 352) and the excreises of Section 1.3, \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W} \mathrm{WW}^{\perp}\). Define \(\mathrm{U}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) by \(\mathrm{U}\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)=v_{1}-r_{2}\), where \(v_{1} \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(v_{2} \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\). Prove that U is a self-adjoint unitary operator.
    5. Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space. A linear operator \(U\) on V is called a partial isometry if there exists a subspace W of V such that \(\|\mathrm{U}(x)\|=\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~W}\) and \(\mathrm{U}(x)=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~W}^{\perp}\). Observe that \(W\) need not be \(U\)-invariant. Suppose that \(U\) is such an operator and \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for W. Prove the following results. (a) \(\langle\mathrm{U}(x), \mathrm{U}(y)\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~W}\). Hint: Use Exercise 20 of Section 6.1. (b) \(\left\{\mathrm{U}\left(r_{1}\right), \mathrm{U}\left(r_{2}\right) \ldots, \mathrm{U}\left(r_{k}\right)\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{U})\). (c) There exists an orthonormal basis \(\gamma\) for V such that the first \(k\) columns of \([U]_{\gamma}\) form an orthonormal set and the remaining columns are zero. (d) Let \(\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{j}\right\}\) be an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}(U)^{\perp}\) and \(\beta=\) \(\left\{\mathrm{U}\left(v_{1}\right), \mathrm{U}\left(v_{2}\right), \ldots, \mathrm{U}\left(v_{k}\right), w_{1}, \ldots, w_{j}\right\}\). Then \(\beta\) is an orthonormal basis for \(V\). (e) Let T be the lincar operator on V that satisfies \(\mathrm{T}\left(\mathrm{U}\left(v_{i}\right)\right)=v_{i}\) \((1 \leq i \leq k)\) and \(T\left(w_{i}\right)=0(1 \leq i \leq j)\). Then \(T\) is well defined, and \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{U}^{*}\). Hint: Show that \(\langle\mathrm{U}(x), y\rangle=\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \beta\). There are four cases. (f) \(\mathrm{U}^{*}\) is a partial isometry.

    This exercise is continued in Exercise 9 of Section 6.6. 21. Let \(A\) and \(B\) be \(n \times n\) matrices that are unitarily equivalent. (a) Prove that \(\operatorname{tr}\left(\Lambda^{*} A\right)=\operatorname{tr}\left(B^{*} B\right)\). (b) Use (a) to prove that

    \[ \sum_{i, j=1}^{n}\left|A_{i j}\right|^{2}=\sum_{i, j=1}^{n}\left|B_{i j}\right|^{2} . \]

    (c) Use (b) to show that the matrices

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & i \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad\left(\begin{array}{ll} i & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right) \]

    are not unitarily equivalent. 22. Let \(V\) be a real inner product space. (a) Prove that any translation on \(V\) is a rigid motion. (b) Prove that the composite of any two rigid motions on \(V\) is a rigid motion on V . 23. Prove the following variation of Theorem 6.22: If \(f: V \rightarrow V\) is a rigid motion on a finite-dimensional real inner product space \(V\), then there exists a unique orthogonal operator T on V and a unique translation \(g\) on \(V\) such that \(f=\mathrm{T} \circ g\). 24. Let \(T\) and \(U\) be orthogonal operators on \(R^{2}\). Use Theorem 6.23 to prove the following results. (a) If \(T\) and \(U\) are both reflections about lines through the origin, then UT is a rotation. (b) If T is a rotation and U is a reflection about a line through the origin, then both UT and TU are reflections about lines through the origin. 25. Suppose that \(T\) and \(U\) are reffections of \(R^{2}\) about the respective lines L and \(\mathrm{L}^{\prime}\) through the origin and that \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) are the angles from the positive \(x\)-axis to \(L\) and \(L^{\prime}\), respectively. By Exercise 24. UT is a rotation. Find its angle of rotation. 26. Suppose that \(T\) and \(U\) are orthogonal operators on \(R^{2}\) such that \(T\) is the rotation by the angle \(\phi\) and U is the reflection about the line L through the origin. Let \(\psi\) be the angle from the positive \(r\)-axis to \(L\). By Exercise 24, both UT and TU are reflections about lines \(L_{1}\) and \(L_{2}\). respectively, through the origin. (a) Find the angle \(\theta\) from the positive \(x\)-axis to \(L_{1}\). (b) Find the angle \(\theta\) from the positive \(x\)-axis to \(\mathrm{L}_{2}\). 27. Find new coordinates \(x^{\prime}\). \(y^{\prime}\) so that the following quadratic forms can be writtell as \(\lambda_{1}\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}\). (a) \(x^{2}+4 x y+y^{2}\) (b) \(2 x^{2}+2 x y+2 y^{2}\) (c) \(x^{2}-12 x y-4 y^{2}\) (d) \(3 x^{2}+2 x y+3 y^{2}\) (e) \(x^{2}-2 x y+y^{2}\) 28. Consider the expression \(X^{t} A X\). where \(X^{t}=(x, y, z)\) and \(A\) is as defined in Exercise 2(c). Find a change of coordinates \(x^{\prime} \cdot y^{\prime}, z^{\prime}\) so that the preceding expression is of the form \(\lambda_{1}\left(x^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\lambda_{2}\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\lambda_{3}\left(z^{\prime}\right)^{2}\). 29. \(Q R\)-Factorization. Let \(w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n}\) be lincarly independent vectors in \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\), and let \(v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\) be the orthogonal vectors obtained from \(u_{1} . u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}\) by the Gram-Schmidt process. Let \(u_{1}, u_{2} \ldots \ldots u_{n}\) be the orthonormal basis obtained by normalizing the \(v_{i} \cdot \mathrm{~s}\). (a) Solving (1) in Section 6.2 for \(w_{k}\) in terms of \(u_{k}\), show that

    \[ u_{k}=\left\|r_{k}\right\| u_{k}+\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\left\langle u_{k}, u_{j}\right\rangle u_{j} \quad(1 \leq k \leq n) \]

    (b) Let \(A\) and \(Q\) denote the \(n \times n\) matrices in which the \(k\) th columns are \(u_{k}\) and \(u_{k}\), respectively. Define \(R \in M_{n \times n}(F)\) by

    \[ R_{j k}= \begin{cases}\left\|v_{j}\right\| & \text { if } j=k \\ \left\langle u_{k}, u_{j}\right\rangle & \text { if } j<k \\ 0 & \text { if } j>k\end{cases} \]

    Prove \(A=Q R\). (c) Compute \(Q\) and \(R\) as in (b) for the \(3 \times 3\) matrix whose columns are the vectors \((1,1,0),(2,0,1)\), and \((2,2,1)\). (d) Since \(Q\) is unitary [orthogonal] and \(R\) is upper triangular in (b), we have shown that every invertille matrix is the product of a mitary [orthogonal] matrix and an upper triangular matrix. Suppose that \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) is invertible and \(A=Q_{1} R_{1}=Q_{2} R_{2}\). where \(Q_{1} . Q_{2} \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) are unitary and \(R_{3}, R_{2} \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) are upper triangular. Prove that \(D=R_{2} R_{1}^{-1}\) is a mitary diagonal matrix. Hint: Use Exercise 17. (e) The \(Q R\) factorization described in (b) provides an orthogonalization method for solving a linear system \(A x=b\) when \(A\) is invertible. Decompose \(A\) to (QR. by the Gram Schmidt process or other means, where \(Q\) is unitary and \(R\) is upper triangular. 'Then \(Q R x=b\), and henee \(R x=Q^{\times} b\). This last system can be easily solved since \(R\) is upper triangular.

    Use the orthogomalization methool and (c) to solve the system

    \[ \begin{aligned} x_{1}+2 r_{2}+2 r_{3} & =1 \\ x_{1}+2 x_{3} & =11 \\ r_{2}+x_{3} & =-1 . \end{aligned} \]
    1. Suppose that 3 aud 9 are ordered bases for an \(n\)-dimensional real [complex] inner product space V. Prove that if \(Q\) is an orthogonal [unitary] \(n \times n\) matrix that changes oroordinates into 3 -coordinates, then \(; 3\) is orthonomal if and only if \(\gamma\) is orthonomal.

    The following definition is used in Exercises 31 and 32 . Definition. Let. V be a finitcodimensional complex [real] inner product space. and let ube a mit vector in V. Definn the Householder operator \(\mathrm{H}_{u}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) bu \(\mathrm{H}_{n}(. r)=r-2\langle r\), u \(\rangle\) u for all \(r \in \mathrm{~V}\). 31. Let \(\mathrm{H}_{u}\) be a Honseholder operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V. Prove the following results. (a) \(H_{u}\) is linear. (b) \(\mathrm{H}_{12}(x)=x\) if and only if \(x\) is orthogomal to \(u\). (c) \(\mathrm{H}_{u}(u)=-u\). (d) \(\mathrm{H}_{u}^{*}=\mathrm{H}_{a}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_{u}^{2}=\mathrm{I}\). and beuce \(\mathrm{H}_{u}\) is a unitary [orthogonal] operator on V . (Note: If V is a ral inner product space, then in the language of Section 6.11. \(\mathrm{H}_{a}\) is a reflection.)

    [^10]32. Let V be a finite-dimensional inner product space over \(F\). Let \(x\) and \(y\) be linearly independent vectors in \(\vee\) such that \(\|x\|=\|y\|\). (a) If \(F=C\), prove that there exists a unit vector \(u\) in \(V\) and a complex number \(\theta\) with \(|\theta|=1\) such that \(\mathrm{H}_{u}(x)=\theta y\). Ifint: Choose \(\theta\) so that \(\langle x, \theta y\rangle\) is real, and set \(u=\frac{1}{\|x-\theta y\|}(x-\theta y)\). (b) If \(F=R\), prove that there exists a unit vector \(u\) in V such that \(\mathrm{H}_{u}(x)=y\).

    6.6 ORTHOGONAL PROJECTIONS AND THE SPECTRAL THEOREM¶

    In this section, we rely heavily on Theorems 6.16 (p. 372) and 6.17 (p. 374) to develop an elegant representation of a normal (if \(F=C\) ) or a self-adjoint (if \(F=R\) ) operator T on a finite-dimensional inerer product space. We prove that \(T\) can be written in the form \(\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\), where \(\lambda_{1} . \lambda_{2} \ldots, \lambda_{k}\) are the distinct eigenvalues of T and \(\mathrm{T}_{1}, \mathrm{~T}_{2}, \ldots \mathrm{~T}_{k}\) are orthogonal projections. We must first develop some results about these special projections.

    We assume that the reader is familiar with the results about direct sums developed at the end of Section 5.2 . The special case where \(V\) is a direct sum of two suthspaces is considered in the exercises of Section 1.3.

    Recall from the exercises of Section 2.1 that if \(V=W_{1} \Phi, W_{2}\), then a linear operator T on V is the projection on \(\mathrm{W}_{1}\) along \(\mathrm{W}_{2}\) if, whenever \(x=x_{1}+x_{2}\). with \(x_{1} \in W_{1}\) and \(x_{2} \in W_{2}\), we have \(T(x)=x_{1}\). By Exercise 26 of Section 2.1. we have

    \[ R(\mathrm{~T})=\mathrm{W}_{1}=\{x \in \mathrm{~V}: \mathrm{T}(x)=x\} \quad \text { and } \quad \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{~T})=\mathrm{W}_{2} . \]

    So \(V=R(T) \oplus N(T)\). Thus there is no ambiguity if we refer to \(T\) as a "projection on \(W_{1}\) " or simply as a "projection." In fact, it can be shown (sce Exercise 17 of Section 2.3) that \(T\) is a projection if and only if \(T=T^{2}\). Because \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{2}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \uparrow \mathrm{~W}_{3}\) doss not imply that. \(\mathrm{W}_{2}=\mathrm{W}_{3}\), we see that \(W_{1}\) does not uniquely determine \(T\). For an orthogonal projection \(T\). howewer. T is uniquely determined by its range.

    Definition. Let V be an imer product space, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) be a projection. We say that \(T\) is an orthogonal projection if \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\) and \(N(T)^{\perp}=R(T)\).

    Note that by Exercise \(13(\mathrm{c})\) of Section 6.2, if V is finite-dimensional. we need only assume that one of the preceding conditions holds. For example, if \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\), then \(R(T)=R(T)^{\perp \perp}=N(T)^{\perp}\).

    Now assume that \(W\) is a finite-dimensional sulspace of an imer product space \(V\). In the notation of Theorem 6.6 (p. 350)), we can define a function \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}\) by \(\mathrm{T}(y)=u\). It is casy to show that T is an orthogonal projection on W. We can say even more -there exists exactly one orthogonal projection on \(W\). For if \(T\) and \(U\) are orthogonal projections on \(W\), then \(R(T)=W=\) \(R(U)\). Hence \(N(T)=R(T)^{\perp}=R(U)^{\perp}=N(U)\), and since every projection is uniquely determined by its range and null space, we have \(T=U\). We call \(T\) the orthogonal projection of V on W .

    To understand the geometric difference between an arbilrary projection on \(W\) and the orthogonal projection on \(W\), let \(V=R^{2}\) and \(W=\operatorname{span}\{(1,1)\}\). Define \(U\) and \(T\) as in Figure 6.5. where \(T(\cdots)\) is the foot of a perpendicular from \(v\) on the line \(y=x\) and \(\mathrm{U}\left(a_{1}, a_{2}\right)=\left(a_{1}, a_{1}\right)\). Then T is the orthogonal projection of \(V\) on \(W\), and \(U\) is a different projection on \(W\). Note that \(r-\mathrm{T}(v) \in \mathrm{W}^{\perp}\), whereas \(v-\mathrm{U}(v) \notin \mathrm{W}^{\perp}\).

    Figure 6.5 From Figure 6.5. we see that \(\mathbf{T}(u)\) is the "best approximation in W to \(u^{\prime}\); that is, if \(w \in \mathrm{~W}\), then \(\|w-v\| \geq\|\mathrm{T}(v)-v\|\). In fact. this approximation property characterizes \(T\). These results follow immediately from the corollary to Theorem 6.6 (p. 3500 ).

    As an application to Fourier analysis, recall the inner product space \(H\) and the orthonormal set \(S\) in Example 9 of Section 6.1. Define a trigonometric polynomial of degree \(n\) to be a function \(g \in \mathrm{H}\) of the form

    \[ g(t)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n} a_{j} f_{j}(t)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n} a_{j} c^{i j t} . \]

    where \(a_{n}\) or \(a_{-n}\) is nonzero. Let \(f \in \mathrm{H}\). We show that the best approximation to \(f\) by a trigonometric polynomial of degree less than or equal to \(n\) is the trigonometric polynomial whose coefficients are the Fourier coefficients of \(f\) relative to the orthonormal set \(S\). For this result, let \(\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{f_{j}:|j| \leq n\right\}\right)\), and let T be the orthogonal projection of H on W. The corollary to Theorem 6.6 (p. 350) tells us that the best approximation to \(f\) by a function in \(W\) is

    \[ \mathrm{T}(f)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n}\left\langle f . f_{j}\right\rangle f_{j} \]

    An algebraic characterization of orthogonal projections follows in the next theorem.

    Theorem 6.24. Let \(V\) be an inner product space, and let \(T\) be a linear operator on V . Then T is an orthogonal projection if and only if T has an adjoint \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) and \(\mathrm{T}^{2}=\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\).

    Proof. Suppose that \(T\) is an orthogonal projection. Since \(T^{2}=T\) because \(T\) is a projection, we need only show that \(T^{*}\) exists and \(T=T^{*}\). Now \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T}) \oplus \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\). Let \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Then we can write \(x=x_{1}+x_{2}\) and \(y=y_{1}+y_{2}\), where \(x_{1}, y_{1} \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(x_{2}, y_{2} \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Hence

    \[ \langle x \cdot \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}+x_{2}, y_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle x_{2}, y_{1}\right\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}\right\rangle \]

    and

    \[ \langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}+y_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle x_{1}, y_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}\right\rangle \]

    So \(\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\); thus \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) exists and \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\). Now suppose that \(T^{2}=T=T^{*}\). Then \(T\) is a projection by Exercise 17 of Section 2.3, and hence we must, show that \(R(T)=N(T)^{\perp}\) and \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\). Let \(x \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Then \(x=\mathrm{T}(x)=\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)\), and so

    \[ \langle x \cdot y\rangle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(r) \cdot y\right\rangle=\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y)\rangle=\langle x \cdot 0\rangle=0 . \]

    Therefore \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\), from which it follows that \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T}) \subseteq \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). Let \(y \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\). We must show that \(y \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\), that is, \(\mathrm{T}(y)=y\). Now

    \[ \begin{aligned} \|y-\mathrm{T}(y)\|^{2} & =\langle y-\mathrm{T}(y), y-\mathrm{T}(y)\rangle \\ & =\langle y \cdot y-\mathrm{T}(y)\rangle-\langle\mathrm{T}(y) \cdot y-\mathrm{T}(y)\rangle . \end{aligned} \]

    Since \(y-\mathrm{T}(y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\), the first term must equal zero. But also

    \[ \left.\langle\mathrm{T}(y), y-\mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=\left\langle y, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y-\mathrm{T}(y))\right\rangle=\langle y \cdot \mathrm{~T}(y-\mathrm{T}(y))\rangle=\langle y \cdot 0\rangle\right\rangle=0 . \]

    Thus \(y-\mathrm{T}(y)=0\); that is, \(y=\mathrm{T}(y) \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\). Hence \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \downarrow\). Using the preceding results, we have \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)^{\perp \perp} \supseteq N(T)\) by Exercise \(13(\mathrm{~b})\) of Section 6.2. Now suppose that \(x \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). For any \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y\rangle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y)\right\rangle=\langle x . \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=0\). So \(\mathrm{T}(x)=0\), and thus \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Hence \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\).

    Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space, \(W\) be a subspace of \(V\), and \(T\) be the orthogonal projection of V on W . We may choose an orthonormal basis \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V such that \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k}\right\}\) is a basis for W . Then \([T]_{\beta}\) is a diagonal matrix with ones as the first \(k\) diagonal entries and zeros elsewhere. In fact, \([T]_{\beta}\) has the form

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{ll} I_{k} & O_{1} \\ O_{2} & O_{3} \end{array}\right) \]

    If U is any projection on W , we may choose a basis \(\gamma\) for V such that \([\mathrm{U}]_{\gamma}\) has the form above; however \(\gamma\) is not necessarily orthonormal.

    We are now ready for the principal theorem of this section. Theorem 6.25 (The Spectral Theorem). Suppose that T is a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V over \(F\) with the distinct eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{k}\). Assume that \(T\) is normal if \(F=C\) and that T is self-adjoint if \(F=R\). For each \(i(1 \leq i \leq k)\), let \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\) be the eigenspace of T corresponding to the eigenvalue \(\lambda_{i}\), and let \(\mathrm{T}_{i}\) be the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\). Then the following statements are true. (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{2} \oplus \cdots \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{k}\). (b) If \(W_{i}^{\prime}\) denotes the direct sum of the subspaces \(W_{j}\) for \(j \neq i\), then \(W_{i}^{\perp}=W_{i}^{\prime}\). (c) \(\mathrm{T}_{i} \mathrm{~T}_{j}=\delta_{i j} \mathrm{~T}_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i, j \leq k\). (d) \(\boldsymbol{I}=\mathrm{T}_{1}+\mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+\mathrm{T}_{k}\). (e) \(\mathrm{T}=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\).

    Proof. (a) By Theorems 6.16 (p. 372) and 6.17 (p. 374), T is diagonalizable; so

    \[ \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{2} \oplus \cdots \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{k} \]

    by Theorem 5.11 (p. 278). (b) If \(x \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~W}_{j}\) for some \(i \neq j\), then \(\langle x, y\rangle=0\) by Theorem \(6.15(\mathrm{~d})\) (p. 371). It follows easily from this result that \(\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime} \subseteq \mathrm{W}_{i}^{\perp}\). From (a), we have

    \[ \operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime}\right)=\sum_{j \neq i} \operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{~W}_{j}\right)=\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{V})-\operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}_{i}\right) \]

    On the other hand, we have \(\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i}^{1}\right)=\operatorname{dim}(V)-\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i}\right)\) by Theorem 6.7(c) (p. 352). Hence \(W_{i}^{\prime}=W_{i}^{\perp}\), proving (b). (c) The proof of (c) is left as an exercise. (d) Since \(\mathrm{T}_{i}\) is the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\), it follows from (b) that \(\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{T}_{i}\right)=\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{T}_{i}\right)^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime}\). Hence, for \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have \(x=\) \(x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots+x_{k}\), where \(\mathrm{T}_{i}(x)=x_{i} \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\), proving (d). (e) For \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), write \(x=x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots+x_{k}\), where \(x_{i} \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\). Then

    \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{T}(x) & =\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}\right)+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2}\right)+\cdots+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{k}\right) \\ & =\lambda_{1} x_{1}+\lambda_{2} x_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} x_{k} \\ & =\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}(x)+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}(x)+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}(x) \\ & =\left(\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\right)(x) . \end{aligned} \]

    The set \(\left\{\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{k}\right\}\) of eigenvalues of \(T\) is called the spectrum of \(T\), the sum \(I=T_{1}+T_{2}+\cdots+T_{k}\) in (d) is called the resolution of the identity operator induced by \(T\), and the sum \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) in (e) is called the spectral decomposition of T . The spectral decomposition of T is unique up to the order of its eigenvalues.

    With the preceding notation, let \(\beta\) be the union of orthonormal bases of the \(W_{i}\) 's and let \(m_{i}=\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i}\right)\). (Thus \(m_{i}\) is the multiplicity of \(\lambda_{i}\).) Then \([T]_{\beta}\) has the form

    \[ \left(\begin{array}{cccc} \lambda_{1} I_{m_{1}} & O & \cdots & O \\ O & \lambda_{2} I_{m_{2}} & \cdots & O \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ O & O & \cdots & \lambda_{k} I_{m_{k}} \end{array}\right) \]

    that is, \([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}\) is a diagonal matrix in which the diagonal entries are the eigenvalues \(\lambda_{i}\) of \(T\), and each \(\lambda_{i}\) is repeated \(m_{i}\) times. If \(\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) is the spectral decomposition of \(T\), then it follows (from Exercise 7) that \(g(\mathrm{~T})=g\left(\lambda_{1}\right) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g\left(\lambda_{2}\right) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g\left(\lambda_{k}\right) \mathrm{T}_{k}\) for any polynomial \(g\). This fact is used below.

    We now list several interesting corollaries of the spectral theorem; many more results are found in the exercises. For what follows, we assume that T is a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V over \(F\).

    Corollary 1. If \(F=C_{2}\), then T is normal if and only if \(\mathrm{T}^{*}=g(\mathrm{~T})\) for some polynomial \(g\).

    Proof. Suppose first that \(T\) is normal. Let \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of \(T\). Taking the adjoint of both sides of the preceding equation, we have \(T^{*}=\bar{\lambda}_{1} T_{1}+\bar{\lambda}_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\bar{\lambda}_{k} T_{k}\) since each \(T_{i}\) is self-adjoint. Using the Lagrange interpolation formula (see page 52 ), we may choose a polynomial \(g\) such that \(g\left(\lambda_{i}\right)=\bar{\lambda}_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i \leq k\). Then

    \[ g(\mathrm{~T})=g\left(\lambda_{1}\right) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g\left(\lambda_{2}\right) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g\left(\lambda_{k}\right) \mathrm{T}_{k}=\bar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\bar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\bar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \]

    Conversely, if \(\mathrm{T}^{*}=g(\mathrm{~T})\) for some polynomial \(g\), then T commutes with \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) since T commutes with every polynomial in T . So T is normal.

    Corollary 2. If \(F=C\), then T is unitary if and only if T is normal and \(|\lambda|=1\) for every eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\).

    Proof. If \(T\) is unitary, then \(T\) is normal and every eigenvalue of \(T\) has absolute value 1 by Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.18 (p. 382).

    Let \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of \(T\). If \(|\lambda|=1\) for every eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\), then by (c) of the spectral theorem,

    \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{TT}^{*} & =\left(\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\right)\left(\bar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\bar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\bar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\right) \\ & =\left|\lambda_{1}\right|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\left|\lambda_{2}\right|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\left|\lambda_{k}\right|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{k} \\ & =\mathrm{T}_{1}+\mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+\mathrm{T}_{k} \\ & =\mathrm{I} . \end{aligned} \]

    Hence \(T\) is unitary. Corollary 3. If \(F=C\) and \(T\) is normal, then \(T\) is self-adjoint if and only if every eigenvalue of \(T\) is real.

    Proof. Let T \(=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of T. Suppose that every eigenvalue of \(T\) is real. Then

    \[ \mathrm{T}^{*}=\bar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\bar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\bar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T} \]

    The converse has been proved in the lemma to Theorem 6.17 (p. 374).

    Corollary 4. Let T be as in the spectral theorem with spectral decomposition \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\). Then each \(T_{j}\) is a polynomial in T.

    Proof. Choose a polynomial \(g_{j}(1 \leq j \leq k)\) such that \(g_{j}\left(\lambda_{i}\right)=\delta_{i j}\). Then

    \[ \begin{aligned} g_{j}(\mathrm{~T}) & =g_{j}\left(\lambda_{1}\right) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g_{j}\left(\lambda_{2}\right) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g_{j}\left(\lambda_{k}\right) \mathrm{T}_{k} \\ & =\delta_{1 j} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\delta_{2 j} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\delta_{k j} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T}_{j} . \end{aligned} \]

    EXERCISES¶

    1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying inner product spaces are finite-dimensional. (a) All projections are self-adjoint.
